title: Chapter 1
body: ﻿The Adventures of the Kitchen Crew

title: One                 At First
body:
The footsteps faded up the stairs. All was quiet in the kitchen. A bedroom door closed.

Then the moaning started.

      "You’re prodding me!"

     "It's not my fault, she put me in the wrong place again!"

     "Well could you try and keep your prongs to yourself - they're sharp!"

     "Sorry but Kneil is scratching my handle, he's been put in the wrong compartment

AND the wrong way round!"  One by one the Kitchen crew had a whinge.

    "My bowl is full of crumbs," (Sally Spoon)

    "I've still got butter smeared all over me!" (Florence Fork)

    "She never gets all the jam off my handle!" (Jenny Jam-Spoon)

    "When will she learn which of us sharp knives goes in which slot – I'm squashed in

this one!" (General Carver)

    "Oh do be quiet!" said Butts the butter knife wearily. "I don't know why you bother to

remark on it - it's the same every night except Friday."

    "Ah Friday! Wonderful Friday!"

    "How many more days to Friday?"

    "What would we do without Fridays?"

    "The hot soapy water, the gentle scrub."

.     "Actually being dried and put away in the right place and the right way round!"

Butts said firmly,

     "We're lucky Madge gets a break on Friday, if only Henry was on kitchen duty every

night."

       There was a moments silence as everyone thought of this Utopia.

       The Kitchen Crew lived in an old farmhouse kitchen where Madge - a rather `airy

fairy' artist - flitted round, housework was not her favourite task. Luckily her husband

Henry, an accountant, cleaned up after her once a week .   Oh the bliss!  Friday nights

were special, the highlight of the week for the badly treated utensils in the kitchen!

You might think that the six members of the fork family would all be the same but you

would be wrong. In fact five of them were more or less the same - Florence, Flora,

Flossie, Fred and Fergus were well matched, and then there was Fillip (with an `F'). Fillip

was different in a very particular way, a way that had far reaching effects on the

otherwise ordinary items of cutlery and kitchen utensils in this particular kitchen

       It was a sad fact that the owners of the kitchen, and indeed of the farmhouse itself

-Madge and Henry - were-to put it kindly- economically minded or to put it in such a

way that anyone could understand, they were mean. Don't get me wrong, they were

lovely people, not at all mean in spirit just in the money area. Henry was, after all, an

accountant and therefore bound by some nameless oath (rather like the Hippocratic Oath

of doctors perhaps) to make the most of every penny  which passed through his hands. In

this he had an uphill struggle, not because of any lack of ability on his part but because in

a moment of madness or overwhelming love he had married Madge.

Madge was a delightful lady; she was a very clever artist who made wonderful

modern sculptures out of ordinary materials that were unfortunately so ahead of their

time that people were wont to gasp in admiration then hastily make their escape with

their wallets and purses firmly closed.

        When Henry realised he was doomed to being the only breadwinner in the house

he was forced to look at his out-goings, forced that is by several letters from the bank in

ever deepening shades of red. He discovered that while he spent what was reasonable,

Madge did not. It seemed to Henry that whatever Madge bought was the opposite of

reasonable. Words like extravagant, over the top, ridiculous, mad and 'what on earth...'

came to his mind - often. And so, after a series of attempts at diplomacy, tact, request and

finally demands that Madge was made to see the error of her ways and promised to

reform and out do Henry in parsimony  and thrift.

        At first, rather surprisingly to Henry, this had the effect of making their money

problems even more  shaky. He couldn't understand it. Beans on toast for dinner four

times a week had taken the place of steak and Scotch salmon so he knew she was trying;

though this did mean that he had spent more on indigestion tablets.

        So Henry started snooping. Of course he wouldn't have called it that. He was

'looking at the difference  between income and outgoings.' Like most men Henry was

happy to interest himself in what interested him and leave the rest to his wife but when he

found that in spite of beans on toast and other  cutbacks Madge was still spending more

money than she should, he decided he had to snoop.

        Once a week on Friday evenings Madge was given a break from her kitchen

duties and told to put her feet up while Henry cooked the  meal, washed up and generally

brought the kitchen back into some sort of order. He quite liked it!

           On Friday evenings, Madge was banished to the sitting room with a glass and a

bottle of sherry; she was also allowed the full use of the remote control. Henry,

meanwhile, prepared to do battle. He would have hated to do the housework every day, if

he had been in charge full time there would not have been much to do as he believed in

dealing with everything as it occurred as in - eat a meal, wash up, put things away. This

was not how Madge worked. She never dealt with anything which could be left until

tomorrow and with everything in sets of six she had three clear days before she ran our of

dishes or cutlery and had to remember where she had last seen the sink. So, for Henry,

Friday night was a sort of enjoyable change. One – look at each item. Two - wash it or

clean it. Three - put it away. In this way he had a great time bringing order to a mess on a

grand scale, a sort of clear up on a par with the job faced by many after the Battle of

Britain or The Blitz. It was now to be an ideal time to snoop i.e. to find where his

carefully managed money was going.

        Quietly he closed the kitchen door, ran hot water - more noisily than usual into the

sink and started to quietly open cupboards and drawers. He was shocked, he was amazed,

he was horrified but at least he had discovered how Madge was still spending his money

like water and yet honestly believed she was saving.

        In the pantry were three  dozen huge tins of baked beans. Unfortunately they were

so large that at the end of the week after four meals of baked beans on toast the rest of the

tinful had to be thrown away as being unfit for humans to eat. The same applied to the

bread - noticeably marked `12 loaves for the price of 10'. If there had been more than the

two of them in the house the plan would have worked well but neither Madge nor Henry

had large appetites! After the pantry had been looked at, understood and put down for a

lecture to Madge on false saving, Henry started on the other cupboards. Here he found six

sets of china. No, that wasn't quite right as he looked more carefully there were five cups,

four plates and three saucers of one set; two plates, five saucers and three cups of another

set and so on. The pattern was repeated, though sadly the pattern of the china was not

repeated often enough. There was not one complete set of anything and he knew neither

he nor Madge were the sort to break china. Even when they threw things at each other it

was usually cushions since both were gentle and peace loving by nature and didn't want

to hurt each other, just maybe make a POINT now and again!

        The drawers were also full of strangely incomplete things. Three napkins, several

tablecloths all either marked or with unravelled hems. Candles,  which had been left in

the sun and were slightly bent, packets of seeds with their plant by dates long ago. And

soap - soap in damaged packets and scarred where it had been badly displayed. Slowly it

dawned on Henry that Madge's idea of saving money was to buy all the bargains she

could find, whether food from the local supermarket or closing down sales end of lines or

the kind of things even Oxfam couldn't sell and finally put in the `Any item 10p' basket.

With a sinking heart Henry knew if he raided Madge’s wardrobe and cupboards in the

bedroom he would find the same things - mismatched pillowcases, dresses with their

hems coming down and towels with unsightly stains you would not want near your face.

'Madge the bargain hunter' was more of a spender than 'Madge the careless'.

       After a silent, thoughtful meal together (Madge didn't notice as she was planning

her next sculpture based on lumps of coal and a few chiffon scarves she had picked up

cheaply at Harrods (only £14.99 each (down from £15)) Henry cleared his throat. He was

a kind man and he was glad about what his misguided wife was trying to do, but it had to

stop.

        "Madge my love," he began. Madge looked at him.

        "Yes sweet pea?"

        "Madge my love, I know how hard you have tried to save money and I am

touched but..." Madge glowed she loved praise.

        "However I think you may have got the wrong end of the stick when it comes to

bargains. I happened to notice one or two things you had bought when I was in the

kitchen tonight and I can't help thinking... you have been... too clever er... too clever in

your bargain hunting!"

            Madge’s face fell. This didn't sound like praise after all.

        "But sweet pea those things were all so cheap it would have been a crime NOT to

buy them!"

        "No my love actually it is not a bargain to but something cheaply IF WE DON'T

REALLY NEED IT!"

        "Oh... but..." even Madge couldn't  argue  with that one.

Henry rushed on, "So I thought we could really have some fun next weekend..." Madge

brightened, Henry was not usually into fun.

        "I thought we could sort out what we really need in the kitchen and put the rest

into a car boot sale. In fact," he rushed on torn between guilt at Madges stricken face and

this very bright, fun idea of his; “whatever we make you can spend on materials for your

sculptures!"

             Madge was won. Henry didn't normally take much interest in her hobby.. He had

found out long ago that if he tried to guess  what a sculpture `was' he was not only way

out but had upset Madge as well. He blushed when he recalled asking if one of her

sculptures was a likeness of the 'Old Curiosity Shop' only to be told by a hurt and snooty

Madge that it was in fact `Sunset over Botany Bay'! He had not tried to guess  again

preferring to ask what it was first and then saying `Ah yes, I see...' very wisely and

knowingly.

        Bringing the kitchen down to basics was not without its problems and

disagreements. Henry thought one set of everything would be enough. Madge protested

that would leave her short when they were all used up and in the sink. Henry said that

would not happen if Madge washed up as she went along. Madge looked at him as if he

had suddenly grown two heads. Very slowly and carefully she explained that this would

mean washing – up, EVERY DAY!  “Yes,“ said Henry  blankly…... "But," wailed

Madge, "that would take so much time I wouldn't have time to sculpt anything!" Henry

toyed with this novel and highly desirable possibility - there was only so much room in

the barn and farm outbuildings after all. But he was a kind man and he did want Madge to

be happy.

          "Don't worry about that," he said quickly, "we'll try it out and see how it goes."

And so it came about that the Kitchen Crew was reduced to one set of everything and

with so much more room available was able to, as it were, stretch and expand and, well,

when the coast was clear move about and generally whoop it up!

        What has this got to do with Fillip (with an F)? Well one of Madges savings had

been to buy cheap cutlery one item at a time. She rather missed the point by driving into

town every day until she had the full set. (This explained the huge amount spent on petrol

which was still baffling Henry). She had bought a knife on Monday, a spoon on Tuesday

and a fork on Wednesday until she had six sets of half a dozen each. Sometime during

these trips the shop had run out of one design and started stocking another very similar

one, Madge had not noticed which was why Fillip (with an F), the last item bought, was

very slightly different from the rest of the cutlery. Fortunately they were a kindly lot and
2
treated him just like the rest of the forks, but there was definitely something different

about him        Fillip remembered the shop. His family ~ the other knives, forks and

spoons had been kidnapped and sent there to be sold. One by one, when no one was

about, the others had legged it and disappeared. Fillip was left on his own. He didn't

know why he hadn't escaped with the others. Perhaps he was lazy or comfortable or had

no idea of where he was bound but he stayed put trying to have the sort of chat with  the

rest of the cutlery in the shop that he had had with his family. But, they were strangely

silent and didn’t reply to Fillip’s attempts.

         At first he put it down to modesty and humility on their part and to being good

listeners but gradually Fillip became bored and decided maybe he should have left with

the others after all. Just as he was planning his escape Madge came into the shop and

bought him. Buried in the bottom of her bag his escape was a non-starter for the time

being but he thought comfortably, "We're probably going somewhere exciting and life

will be more interesting!"

        Which was how he came to be in Madge's kitchen, dumped from her shopping

bag into a drawer containing six knives, six spoons and five forks apparently all of the

same design as Fillip.

        After waiting politely and in vain for someone to start a  conversation Fillip took

it upon himself to begin.

        "Good morning," he began brightly, "I'm Fillip (with an F), how do you do?"

There was silence. Puzzled, Fillip tried again.

        "What's it like living here then?" Silence. For the first time it dawned on Fillip

that the other cutlery was not exactly like him. He, Fillip, was lively (not alive you

understand, he didn't eat or drink, he didn't have to go to school and he hadn't opened a

bank account or saved up for a foreign holiday). The rest of the cutlery was simply –

cutlery. Fillip was disappointed. He started to fidget which was how he managed to touch

the knife next to him with his left foot and his right hand. The knife stirred, twitched and

opened one sleepy eye.

        "What's the time?" it said. Fillip was delighted, someone to talk to at last.

        "It's time to get going!" said Fillip. "I'm Fillip with an F, who are you?”

        "I think I'm Kneil with a K," said the knife hesitantly.

            The rest of the cutlery remained inert, lifeless and lively less. Fillip experimented.

He touched a nearby spoon with his left hand. Nothing. Right hand. Nothing. Right hand

and left foot - ah! Success! The spoon shivered and opened her eyes and said,

          "Hello, I'm Suzy!" One by one as Fillip moved around the drawer the items of

cutlery were made lively and introduced themselves. The knife family - Knigel, Kneil,

Knorma, Knita and Knoreen. The spoon family whose names all began with 'S', the fork

family whose names all began with 'F' all introduced themselves and started to chatter.

They were very self interested Fillip found and soon became bored with the spoons,

           "Is my bowl really shiny, I can see myself in yours but can you see yourself in

mine?"

            The forks compared prongs and the knives were concerned about being sharp

enough to do the job properly but not sharp enough to be a danger to anyone.

        Fillip climbed out of the half open drawer (Madge never closed any drawers or

cupboard doors, she reasoned it saved her time in the long run, time which she could

spend sculpting). He looked around for someone interesting to enliven and spotted a

wooden block with sharp kitchen knives slotted into it.

        "Ah," he thought, "they could be distant relatives." Gingerly he climbed up the

block, right hand, left foot went out to the largest knife. It shuddered and with a sucking

sound pulled its head out of the slot. General Carver flexed his weedy arms and twirled

his large moustache. His bushy eyebrows twitched as he looked around the kitchen with

big poppy eyes. He caught sight of Fillip.

        "Ah - a dinner fork!" he said, "and I suppose that means there are dinner knives

around?"

        "Yes," said Fillip, "in that drawer."

        "Ah, hum," said the General. "Good to have some foot soldiers around, but I

really need to have my staff made lively so jump to it young man, get the rest of the

officers on their feet.“

            Fillip was overawed by the sheer size of the General and found himself standing

taller and saluting,

           "Yes sir, right away sir!"

           Carefully placing right hand and left foot in position he set about enlivening

Colonel Breadknife, Captain Boning, Major Fruit and Veg, and as an afterthought he

added Sergeant All-Purpose to the troop. As each straightened and inspected himself and

the others Fillip realised that here was another group of self centered cutlery. With much

twisting and twirling they inspected their own and each others blades for sharpness.

Mottos started to be thrown about and taken up by the others.

        "East, west sharps best!",

        "You're nothing without an edge!",

        "A blades life is the only life!".

        Fillip left them to their parading and looked around for someone more interesting to

enliven. Next to the cooker he spotted a box with several wooden spoons sticking up.

Once again he used his right hand and left foot and once again another group of kitchen

utensils became lively. Unfortunately they too were a little introverted and concerned

only with themselves and each other.

        "Wood you believe it!" said Holly. "There's a puddle in the box and my end is

getting mouldy!"

           "Look at my bowl," said Olive "it's so dry it’s going to split soon!"

           "I woodn't worry," said Mable Maple, the smallest, "a good old stir fry will take

care of that but I have a splinter! I woodn't have thought it possible, goodness knows how

I'm going to get rid of that!"

        Fillip turned away in disgust. Was everyone in this kitchen only interested in

themselves? Then he spotted another knife, it was different from his set and it wasn't

fierce like the Sharps army. It lay there looking rather innocent and gentle. Fillip applied

his special touch. For a while nothing happened then the knife sat up and looked around

him.

        "Thanks," he said to Fillip, "that feels really good." Fillip was surprised and

pleased. This was the first thanks he had heard after all the enlivening he had done.

        "What’s your name?" he asked.

        "I'm Butts Butterknife," was the reply.

        "Why are you so different from the rest?" asked Fillip.

        "Am I?" said Butts. "Maybe because I'm a vegetarian pacifist! I don't have a sharp

edge, I don't need one, I'm just a butter spreader. I hate the thought of cutting into

anything."

        "You've got a wooden handle that looks different,” said Fillip.

        Butts shuddered. "Yes," he said. "I remember when I was new someone left me

on the cooker and my plastic handle melted. Because I was so useful someone made me

this smart new handle. I do believe it's unique!" Butts looked away modestly but he was

rather proud of his unusual feature. Fillip liked Butts. He was the first enlivened one to

actually want to talk to his enlivener.

        "Come and meet my girlfriend," he said. "Her name is Knoeline (with a K).”

                                                                       13

title: Danger
body:
After Henry’s clearing of the kitchen there was much more room for everyone.  However,

this was of no real use because of Madge.  Madge was good at  saving  space if not

money and why go to the bother of hunting for what you wanted through several drawers

when you could cram everything into one – just.  So while there were empty drawers

aplenty, the utensils jostled together and got in each other’s way in one drawer.

Fortunately the characters of the kitchen owners Madge and Henry prevailed and they

were gentle, kindly folk who, when  the odd spat occurred, were horrified and did all they

could to make up to the others for their shortcomings. So the Kitchen Crew, while

moaning one minute, would be offering to move over to give someone else more space

the next.  Above all this pushing and shoving was the Sharp Army, they had their own

wooden block with a neat slot for each knife – but they also had their problems.  Madge

had somehow not cottoned on to the fact that the best place for a big knife was a big slot.

So poor General Carver was constantly being humiliated by being shoved into Sergeant

All Purpose’s little slot and Sergeant All Purpose could get quite above himself when put

into Colonel Breadknife’s and had to be reminded that finding yourself in a big slot did

not mean you had been promoted.

       And so it came about that Sergeant All Purpose was the one to whom shame came in

the shape of Madge in a hurry. Not that Madge was ever calm and collected – or indeed

in possession of any of the other abilities which Henry liked and which made for a

sensible, well ordered life. .Madge was in a hurry because she had just had a brilliant idea

for her latest sculpture  She had been to an Andy Warhol exhibition and was impressed

by his soup can paintings.  But she thought she could go one better and make a sculpture

using actual soup cans, bean tins and any other rubbish (sorry, artistic material) she could

find. So dinner had to be prepared more quickly then usual and since it wasn’t a beans on

toast night that meant potatoes and veg had to be prepared.  General Carver was no use

when it came to peeling potatoes so Madge found herself using Sergeant All Purpose

(next best thing to Captain Fruit and Veg who fumed in frustration from his temporary

resting place in the cutlery drawer).

          So Sergeant All Purpose was used to peel the potatoes and carrots – no problem

there – and then to cut them into the saucepan.  Not being quite the right shape he

skidded on a carrot and sailed up into the air.  Madge followed his journey in wonder and

then made a bad mistake.  The Kitchen Crew saw it coming, there was a silent combined

gasp – but Madge did not notice– something was falling – catch it.  “No,” they cried,

          “Catch a falling star (if you must) but never, ever catch a falling knife!”  Too late,

to his undying shame and misery Sergeant All Purpose bit deeply into Madge’s hand.

          There was blood everywhere, except in Madge’s face which was rapidly whitening.

Henry heard her scream and came running.  He applied a clean tea towel and led her

gently into the sitting room.  When she was seated and surrounded by cushions, her feet

placed on a stool.  Henry hunted out the First Aid Kit and looked at the damage.  It was a

nasty long cut but not too deep and would not need stitching, just a big plaster and some

TLC.

“Well that’s you out of the kitchen for a while,” said Henry (with mixed feelings), “and

your sculpture will have to wait for a while too.”

          Madge was shocked, she could live without the kitchen quite easily, but her

sculptures were another matter.  In spite of never having sold anything she lived in hope

of being discovered and being exhibited in the Tate Modern, perhaps between an unmade

bed and a pile of bricks.

          So it was that for the Kitchen Crew every night was Friday night as Henry took

over the cooking. They loved it.  Crumbs were cleared out of the drawers; the utensils

were spread through several drawers and stretched out, appreciating the space and the

cleanliness.

          There was no moaning, no scratching, no bad feeling about anything.  It became

very quiet in the kitchen.  The Sharp Army was introduced to Sid the Knife Sharpener

who had been hidden in the back of a drawer surrounded by tablecloths because Madge

had forgotten she’d bought him and didn’t know what he was for anyway.  The Sharp

Army could be rightly proud of their edges and had never been happier.  They took to

marching around in formation chanting.

          “We are the sharpest, the brightest, we are the cleverest and the rightest,” (their

grammar hadn’t improved).

          The wooden spoons were smoothed and oiled, Fillip and his family were not only

washed in hot soapy water and rinsed but also dried and put away - THE SAME DAY!

The forks were together in the fork compartment, the knives in the knife compartment

and the spoons in the spoon compartment.  Peace reigned and everyone was happy – at

first.

           It became very quiet.

           “It’s very nice to be looked after properly,” said Sadie Spoon, “I’ve never had

such a shiny bowl.”

          After a long pause Fred Fork said,

          “There’s absolutely no gunge between my prongs.”

           After an even longer pause someone said,

          “What day is it?”

          “Saturday,” said someone else after some thought.

          There was horror in the kitchen.

         “What happened to Friday?” said Knorma Knife “We used to look forward to it so

much, now every day is like Friday and it’s not the same – why not?”

            Butts coughed timidly,” I have an idea,” he said, “We used to look forward to

Friday because it was different, like an oasis in the desert.  Now every day is the same it’s

kind of --- well – eh – boring!”

            There were murmurs of agreement

            “Who’d have thought it?” said Holly Wood.

            “I woodn’t,” said Olive Wood.

            “I don’t actually like it, “ said Knoeline, “I used to find myself next to Fillip

sometimes now I never do!”

            “I have a confession to make,” said Captain Breadknife, “at first I thought having

Henry not Madge in the kitchen would be bliss – I even thought – you know – eh – well –

perhaps we could arrange to keep her out more often – not wait for an accident!”

          There was a stunned silence as they all thought about this novel idea.  They

examined it and one by one let it go. Butts spoke for everyone,

           “We couldn’t do that to Madge,” he said, “I know she’s untidy and disorganised

but she’s a kind lady – and,” he hesitated and then added with a rush of embarrassment,”

I like the way she talks to me when she uses me!”  There was a chorus of agreement.

         “Yes, yes, she’s very sweet,”

         “Very sociable,”

           “Very considerate.”

           Sergeant All Purpose had said nothing but now and again they could all hear a

muffled sob coming from his slot in the knife block.

           “I’m so ashamed!” he wailed, “I’ll never forgive myself for hurting such a dear,

sweet lady!”

            “Don’t keep on son,” said General Carver gruffly, “It was an accident, we all saw

it coming but couldn’t stop it – you were a victim of circumstance – and of Madge of

course.”

           It was a few days later, the Kitchen Crew had almost given up getting lively after

hours and were lying in their allotted places trying not to be bored rigid.  The Kitchen

door was flung open and a flurry of scarves and beads whirled into the Kitchen.

            “Hello darlings, have you missed me?”  It was Madge, no longer bound and

bandaged and ready to do her worst again.  Silently the Kitchen Crew cheered and

stamped their feet prepared to be neglected and abused and to look forward to Friday.

Things would soon be back to normal, it was good to be lively and to have something to

talk about.

                                                                     17

title: The Present
body:
The Kitchen Crew became aware of an unusual amount of activity in their home.   Madge

was shopping as never before and Henry was not objecting.  Indeed with every new

supermarket bag, bursting at the seams, which arrived in the Kitchen he would smile

kindly at his wife and merely “tut, tut” mildly.  What’s more Madge was putting her

purchases away immediately!  It was normal for plastic carrier bags to be brought in and

forgotten about for several days.  Sometimes it was only a strong smell that alerted Henry

to look through them and to remonstrate with Madge that the use by date was there for a

reason and not just to fill a gap on the label.

          By dint of listening very hard and sharing the scraps of information thus gleaned

the Kitchen Crew were able to piece together what was going on.

          “It’s their 5th Wedding Anniversary on Friday,” said Kathy Knife.

          “It’s Madge’s birthday on Saturday,” said Flora Fork.

          “It’s Henry’s birthday on Thursday,” said Sadie Spoon.  Obviously the females of

the species, even cutlery, had a keener ear for gossip than the males.

            “So what?” said Knoel Knife,

            “So what?” the females sighed

         “They’re going to celebrate of course,” said Kitty, Flossie and Sarah together.

            “Friday night,” said Knoeline  “Henry’s going to cook a special meal for them

and four guests.  “He let Madge choose all the ingredients – she was allowed to spend as

much as she liked for once but she is going to relax while Henry does the cooking.”

            On Friday the doorbell rang before Madge was up and Henry brought a heavy

wooden parcel into the kitchen.  He stowed it away in a fairly empty cupboard that had so

far not been restocked by Madge.  He looked very smug and pleased with himself.

            During the day Madge flitted in and out of the kitchen only to be shown out by

Henry who insisted that the menu was a secret and she should not try to find out what he

was cooking and spoil the surprise.  With everything roasting and simmering nicely

Henry turned his attention to the table.  Back and forth he went to the dining room.  First

with the best lace tablecloth and napkins, then the crystal glasses were polished, held up

to the light then polished again.  Flowers were arranged and candles matched to candle

holders and colour schemes.  The cutlery waited patiently, rather excited that they would

all be in use at the same time for once.  Time was getting on, why had Henry not laid the

cutlery out on the festive table yet?  They were not to be kept  wondering  much longer.

            Henry called Madge into the kitchen.  “My dear,” he said, “I have been very

impressed with the way you have economised since our little – eh – chat.  And because

of that, and your birthday and our anniversary I would like you to have this present and

use them tonight.”  With that he took out the heavy box which he had hidden away earlier

in the day. With obvious excitement Madge opened the wrapping paper and then the box.

            “Oh Henry!” she gasped, “they’re beautiful and how very fitting – silver cutlery

for our lovely party.”  She picked up the box and together they went to the dining room to

put the finishing touches to the table.  In the Kitchen there was a stunned silence.

            “No wonder he didn’t use us, they’ve got something much more glamorous for

their special night,” said Fillip.

            “And maybe not just for tonight!” said Fergus darkly.  “They might decide they

like all that flashy, shiny silver, and use it all the time.”

           “But we’re OK for everyday,” said Spencer tentatively.

           “You know Henry’s policy,” said Knigel – “You don’t need more than one set of

anything – it’s only a matter of time before we end up in a car boot sale!”

             It was a gloomy evening in the Kitchen.  Every now and then the door would

open when Henry came into the Kitchen with dirty dishes or to fetch something.  From

the dining room came the sound of talking and laughter and every now and then odd

clanging noises and yelps.  With every visit Henry seemed to be getting more and more

exasperated and seemed to give out a sort of grim determination.  The Kitchen Crew was

puzzled. The party sounded like a great success if the laughter and amount of chatter

were anything to go by. The number of empty wine bottles was also a sign of a

thoroughly good time being had by one and all.

            At last they heard the sound of noisy goodbyes and manic laughter as the guests

staggered off.  Fortunately they all lived near and didn’t have to drive.

Henry and Madge brought the rest of the party food and dirty plates out to the Kitchen.

There was a certain awkward silence from Henry and more twittering than usual from

Madge.  The sink was loaded and hot soapy water lavished over the dirty dishes.  The

Kitchen crew listened glumly to the familiar sounds wishing they were part of the Friday

night ritual.  Saddly  they remembered Henry’s gentle but thorough washing and rinsing

and drying and PUTTING AWAY.  No one had the heart to say anything as they waited

for the inevitable culling of unnecessary kitchen equipment.

           Madge came into the Kitchen just as Henry was drying his hands she sat at the

kitchen table and fiddled with her scarf.

            “I’m so sorry Henry,” she said. “They are so very beautiful, it’s such a shame.”

            “It’s not your fault my dear,” said Henry kindly.  “It was the same for all of us –

they’re just not very well balanced.  And, something I didn’t realise, they’ll need cleaning

and polishing every time they’re used” Madge looked shocked.

             “But it’s such a waste,” she said sadly “Can you send them back?”

             Henry looked embarrassed,

            “Eh no – actually they were in a sale and are non-returnable.”

            Madge sighed, “They’re such a pretty pattern and I love silver,” she said “but

what can you do with silver cutlery that is too heavy to stay on the plate and takes hours

to clean?”

            It was then that Henry had one of the best ideas of his idea deprived life.  He was

so excited that at first he could hardly speak.  He turned an unhealthy shade of purple and

Madge thought he was going to have a heart attack.

           Slowly he sat at the table next to her and took her hand.

           “My dear,” he started, “I am disappointed that my anniversary present appears to

have been a disaster.  I hope our friends will be able to laugh tomorrow about the bruised

toes and custard spattered clothes but I think YOU can save the day.”

            “I can save something?” said Madge bemused.  Destroy, wreck, ruin and

annihilate were words more familiar to her then SAVE.

            Henry was getting excited again, he seemed to be enjoying keeping Madge in

suspense.

            “What I wanted was to buy you something beautiful which would last and always

be a reminder to you of how much I love you.  Well as cutlery, the silver set was a

disaster but if you can use them to make a wonderful SILVER SCULPTURE it can take

pride of place in the living room and we will see it every day not just when we have a

party!  What do you think?”

            Madge stared at him, her mouth half open, tears welling in her eyes,

           “Oh Henry!” she said  “I could make a beautiful sculpture – but the best

anniversary present and birthday present rolled into one is the compliment you just paid

me!  You have never before taken any interest in my work and now you have given me

my first commission!”

            Henry smiled weakly and patted her hand. He had salvaged the situation and

made his wife happy – he would just have to live with the silver horror on his sideboard.

There were after all nearly a hundred pieces in the set, with a bit of luck it would take her

a very long time to complete.  Older and wiser and hand in hand they closed the kitchen

door and went to bed.

           In the Kitchen, the Kitchen Crew went mad with joy.  The Sharp Army sharpened

themselves to a dangerous degree.  The Knife, Fork and Spoon family did a jig of victory

all over the place and took some time to sort themselves into the right compartments. The

wooden spoons did a conga round the sink and Butts Butterknife went round shaking

everybody’s hand.  Eventually, tired but happy they settled down into their Henry

dictated places and waited for the morning and whatever Madge had in store for them.

Liveliness lives on.

title: Trevor
body:
Madge and Henry had two grown up children, Henry’s son Trevor and Madge’s daughter

Fiona.  They had left home promptly the day after their eighteenth birthdays, just one

year apart.  They left home with speed and a certain pretend reluctance. They loved their

parents very much but if forced would have to admit that living with Madge and Henry

drove them to the wall – up it and over the top.

            Funnily enough it was probably because they were so like their parents that they

couldn’t live with them.  Trevor was like his stepmother, always untidy, absent- minded

and vague but then he was a computer whiz kid.  His work, which involved sorting out

other people’s computer problems, meant he had to be organised, focussed and precise.

So it was more than could be expected that he would carry these qualities home from

work. At home he made up for the restrictions of his work and let it all hang out.  It hung

out literally from his drawers and cupboard and sometimes from his windows.  His

2personal hygiene was impeccable as he sometimes forgot whether he had showered and

so did it again to be on the safe side.  His clothes were a different matter.  Had he lived in

an age when office workers wore suit and ties he would probably have come unstuck

(especially from his job) in the first week. As it was his strange clothing was accepted by

his colleagues as proof that he was a free spirit, with a mind of his own and no fashion

sense.

            He earned a good salary and much of it went on the latest clothes but somehow

they didn’t really go together and this gave him the appearance of someone who had

dressed hurriedly in a darkened room after a shout of  “Fire!”

           Trevor liked to lie in bed as long as possible, he had breakfast the night before to

save time and set his alarm to snooze an hour before he had to get up.  The result was that

he had to wake up several times more often than most people which sometimes made him

forget which day of the week it was. This only mattered if he found himself in work on a

Saturday.  But since no one else was in he was able to sneak back to his flat and no one

was any the wiser about this eccentricity of his.

           His parents were very fond and proud of Trevor.  Henry because he saw in Trevor

the brilliance which enabled him to get a good job straight from university and Madge

because his untidiness and vagueness reminded her of someone she knew, though she

couldn’t quite remember who.

           Trevor ‘phoned home often, he was sometimes on for an hour or more and ran up

huge bills. It wasn’t because he had a lot to say, his calls consisted of some very long

pauses while he was passed from mother to father and all three tried to think of things to

say about three fairly ordinary, unexciting lives.  Trevor didn’t mind the pauses or the

bills – it made up for him not going home very often. He could have visited his step-

mother if she had been on her own quite easily.  They were kindred spirits with little

concern for the more ordinary things of life like food, clothing and being able to find

what you needed inside an hour.  No, it was Henry who was Trevor’s problem.  He

admired and respected his father, he even loved him but Henry had never admitted that

Trevor was grown up and in charge his own life.  As far as Henry was concerned Trevor

came home to stock up on advice and Henry was determined to cram as much as possible

into the few brief hours when Trevor visited.

           As a result Trevor avoided weekends when Henry was off work and occasionally

took a day off midweek to see Madge. Henry accepted that his son had a hectic social life

at weekends and even envied him having the sort of life he, Henry had never quite

managed.  In fact Trevor did not socialise much.  Oh, he made arrangements to meet

friends and even remembered to turn up sometimes but more often than not he forgot

where he was going or at what time or even who he was meeting. In this way he had lost

several girlfriends and two cats.  But he lived in hope of better things to come. He was

after all an optimist and cheerful with it.  So he was genuinely delighted when glancing

down at his socks in response to office sniggers he found one was blue and one red and

KNEW for sure he had another pair just like them in the drawer at home.

          Strangely enough it was all because of Trevor that Butts Butterknife fell hopelessly

in love.

           If their personalities were very different so were their drinking habits. Henry liked

to savour a glass of red wine in the sitting room, Madge drank mainly white wine in the

bath and Trevor preferred bottled beer in the kitchen. Having warned his mother he was

going to make a flying visit Trevor duly turned up one Wednesday lunchtime on his

friend’s motorbike, Trevor had a perfectly serviceable modern car but of this his parents

were unaware.  When he had left home some years before he had had a motorbike and

had therefore been sadly unable to take with him his mothers parting gift of a special

sculpture made up of some computer parts discarded by her son as from the dark ages and

incorporating one of his less favoured ties – an Xmas present from a misguided and

colour blind aunt.  To ensure that he never had room to take this hideous sculpture away

                                                                     24
with him Trevor had to borrow  the  motorbike – something he never forgot to do even

with his poor memory and forgetfulness.

            So it was that they were sitting in the kitchen having a pre lunch drink.  Madge

had retrieved her bottle of Spanish wine from the bathroom and Trevor having found a

bottle of beer at the back of the pantry was now hunting through the drawer for a bottle

opener.

         “These drawers are so empty, Mum, why can’t I find a bottle opener?”

           “I don’t know dear, we never use one, perhaps it went to the car boot sale in the

GRAND CLEAR OUT.  But why do you need one, you used to take the top off with your

teeth!”

            “Last time I did that I chipped a tooth so now I use a bottle opener.”

            “You could try smashing the top off like they do in a Western.”

            “No thanks I don’t fancy a beer and glass cocktail.”

            Madge thought for a moment,

            “I know,” she said, your father’s corkscrew is a bottle opener as well. It’s in the

sitting room, I’ll get it.”  She brought the strange looking object back, opened Trevor’s

bottle and tossed the opener into the cutlery drawer.

           Fillip had been dozing when he was rudely awakened by the arrival on top of him

of a shiny and heavy chrome corkscrew cum bottle opener.  Instinctively his arms and

legs flew up and he touched it with the enlivening right hand and left foot. An enormous

hiccup came from the object,

            “Ta, ever so, very kind, I’m sure – I’m Topsy – who are you?”  I’m Fillip, Why
.
are you hiccupping?”

            “It’s my environment stupid,” she replied woozily.  “I’ve either got me feet in

wine or me ‘ed in beer – s’not my fault!”

           Struggling out from underneath them both came Butts Butterknife.  He looked, he

saw, he fell madly in love with this exotic creature. Then Topsy beamed at him,

            “Ello flat face, who‘re you then?”

            “I’m Butts,” said Butts, “Pleased to meet you, I’m sure.”

            They spent a happy afternoon catching up on their lives so far.  With Madge and

Trevor in an alcoholic haze they didn’t have to worry about being overheard and Madge

was used to hearing voices in strange places anyway.

           Trevor left after two bottles of beer and a large black coffee, apologising once

again for not being able to take his mother’s wonderful sculpture and begging her to look

after it for him.  Henry returned and found he had missed an opportunity to give Trevor

some advice on investing his salary on the Stock Market.  Taking his bottle of red wine

from the cocktail cabinet he hunted in vain for his corkscrew. Exasperated he asked

Madge what she had done with it.  Hastily Madge returned to the kitchen, wrenched

Topsy from Butts arms and took her back to Henry.  The evening progressed pleasantly

enough except that there were now two broken hearts in the house.  Topsy’s in the sitting

room and Butts' in the kitchen.

            “I can’t wait until Trevor visits before I see Topsy again,” moaned the love sick

Butts, “What can I do?”  Fillip racked his brains trying to think how he could help his

friend.

                                                                       26
            He knew Butts and Topsy belonged together because they had been the only two

to thank him for enlivening them.  But what could he do?  He only knew the kitchen.  He

didn’t know where Topsy might be, though he suspected it would be where Henry

opened his wine and was therefore in the sitting room.  He wasn’t even sure if his

liveliness extended beyond the kitchen and the dining room.

           Fillip couldn’t bear to see his friend so unhappy so he made up his mind.

           “Tonight,” he said, “when Madge and Henry have gone to bed we’ll do …

something.”

           The Kitchen door was always left open so that was one problem solved, but once

out in the corridor they were faced with an array of doors, all closed.  They hesitated, not

sure which way to go first then softly down the corridor to the left they heard a familiar

sound from the room at the end,

            “Hic!” it went and “Hic!” again.

           “That sounds like Topsy,” said Butts and set off towards his beloved.  The door

was closed. Baffled the would-be rescuers stopped.  “There is quite a gap underneath,”

said Fillip, “I think we can squeeze underneath.”  They were fortunately quite slim

though

            Fillip had a bit of bother with his curved prongs but the carpet was soft and he

managed to press himself down into it and get under.  Once inside the sitting room they

looked around.  Topsy was lying on the floor next to an empty bottle.  Between hiccups,

she was singing softly to herself,

            “Frere Jacques, Frere Jacques …… son et la matina …”

            “French?” said Butts in surprise, “I thought she was a cockney!  Are you sure

that’s Topsy?”

            Fillip thought for a moment then light dawned.

            “I know what it is!” he grinned.  “When you met her she had just opened Trevor’s

beer but in the sitting room she had opened Henry’s wine!   In other words she’s a split

personality!  Can you cope with that do you think Butts?”

           Butts gulped and then said stoically, “Of course I can – it’s her I love, not the way

she speaks! So lets get on with it and rescue her from a lonely life in the sitting room.

           Together they advanced on Topsy.  She was surprised to see them.

           “Allo mon chere Butts,” she cried and making a drunken lunge at him hugged him

tight.

          “You ‘ave come to rescue me, my ‘ero!”

           Together Fillip and Butts manhandled her across the room to the door.  Then they

stopped, baffled.  Because of Topsy’s shape –she had a wide circle of metal to fit over a

bottle top- there was no way they could get her under the door! How were they going to

get her out, when they couldn’t possibly open the door themselves?  They looked around

the room.  As well as Topsy’s gentle hiccupping there was another noise.  It was coming

from an armchair next to a radiator and sounded like a quiet motor running.  Nervously

Fillip and Butts eased over to the chair and hauled themselves up.  Ensconced in cushions

was a large tabby cat.

           “Of course,” said Fillip, “It’s Crumpet, Madge’s cat.  Maybe he’ll help us.” With

difficulty they woke Crumpet up. He was not amused.

           “What?” he said grumpily, having been woken from a dream of mouse hunting

 that had promised to knock all previous dreams into a cocked-hat.

                 “We need your help Crumpet,” said Fillip and explained the need to have the door opened.

           “So,” said Crumpet carefully, “Let me get this straight, you want me to yowl so

Henry will come down and throw me out in the cold?  In your dreams Fillip, get lost!”

           Frustrated Fillip and Butts climbed down while Crumpet tried to pick up the

threads of his dream.  Fillip looked around for inspiration and spotted a half empty bottle

of wine on a coffee table in front of the stone fireplace.

           “You’ll have to help me Butts,” he said “I think we can still get Henry down here

and nip out while the door is open.  They climbed on to the coffee table and with much

puffing and panting managed to push the wine bottle to the edge.  It tottered for a

moment then with a final push it fell onto the hearth with a resounding crash.  Crumpet,

rudely awakened for the second time, looked out from his nest of cushions to see what

was going on.  He wasn’t a stupid cat, just lazy.  He quickly put two and two together and

realised HE would get the blame and had better be ready to make himself scarce as soon

as Henry appeared.  Meanwhile the trio hid behind the door and waited.

           Sure enough there were ponderous footsteps on the stairs and Henry armed with a

poker put his head cautiously round the door, ready for a possible burglar to thump him.

Crumpet shot out between his legs and headed for the cat flap.  Henry could also add two

and two together but made five – blaming the cat for the crash, just as Crumpet knew he

would.  As Henry bent down to clear the shards of glass the real culprits slipped through

the open door, supporting the tipsy Topsy and made their way back to the kitchen.

            There were cheers from the Kitchen Crew as they related their adventure and

Topsy was welcomed into the gang.

             It would be nice to say that the all lived happily ever after but this was a little too

soon. The next evening Henry hunting around for his corkscrew finally found it in the

kitchen and puzzled took it back to the sitting room.  Butts and Topsy were

heart broken again.

           It took three more rescue operations before Henry finally gave in and bought

himself a brand new modern corkscrew and left Topsy at peace in the kitchen.

           “Though,” he did say a little peevishly to Madge, “I don’t see why someone as

untidy as yourself should suddenly take to putting things away – and in the kitchen, of all

places?”
             Madge laughed airily – she didn’t think she had put anything away!  Perhaps

she was coming down with some strange illness that made you act out of character?  She

put her hand to for forehead but did not think she had a temperature so, as she was want

to do, she put it out of her mind and started planning a new sculpture. Topsy continued to

intrigue Butts by sounding alternately cockney and French depending on what she had

been used for.  But Butts didn’t mind her mood swings – after all love is deaf as well as

blind.

title: The Would be Thief
body:
Madge was a very soft hearted lady.  She was always won by hard luck stories and never

suspected anyone could be other than they seemed or that they might try to cheat her.

           So when a young man came to her door selling bits and bobs Madge not only

bought things she didn’t need (she would later explain to Henry that it was like giving to

charity) but invited the young man in for a beer.

           Nick was enthusiastic about her kitchen, stylish he called it without specifying

which style.  His enthusiasm gave him the opportunity to look round very thoroughly

indeed and Madge, so flattered by his interest, did not notice the cold, darting eyes that

missed nothing.  He sat at the kitchen table and waited his chance.  Two things he

noticed, Madge’s open handbag on the table with her purse clearly visible and an

interesting parcel by the door waiting to be posted.

           The Kitchen Crew was in the usual sleepy state they adopted when humans were

around but they missed nothing that was going on and watched the young man

suspiciously.  They were ready to leap into action if need be though they were not clear

what might happen or how a few knives, forks and spoons could intervene in any useful

way.  But they were keyed up and ready.

           It was Fillip who first noticed Nicks interest in Madge’s handbag, the way he kept

leaning towards it when Madge wasn’t looking as though he was trying to see right

inside. “I wonder if he’s going to snatch her purse?”  Fillip muttered to Butts who was

sitting in a jam jar next to him, they were waiting for Madge to remember to dry them

and put them away but they didn’t hold out much hope and could already feel the water

stains drying on them.  Nick wasn’t getting very far Madge was flitting as usual and

although she wasn’t consciously watching Nick she kept looking round just as he was

reaching out a hand to her bag.

          It suddenly became clear to Fillip that Nick was not after the purse which probably

only contained change.  Barely visible was an object made of green leather – Madge’s

wallet and potentially a more rewarding haul than her change purse.  It would take Nick

longer to pull it out than Madge was giving him at the moment and you could almost see

the cogs of his brain turning as he tried to think of a way to give himself enough time to

winkle it out of the handbag and into his pocket.

             Fillip and Butts who, at least together, were brighter than Nick, realised what he

was planning and agreed he had to be stopped, but what could they do?  Fillip looked

around for inspiration and spotted Laddie Ladle leaning against a wall deep in thought.

He was only deep in one thought because Laddie only ever thought of one thing and that

was the bliss of getting his head down into a saucepan of steaming hot winter stew.  He

lived for nothing else and when sometimes weeks went by without a stew being prepared

Laddie got very depressed and frustrated and felt useless – something he was always

moaning to the others about.

            “I feel useless,” he would say to anyone who would listen, “Frustrated and

useless. Here I am day in day out with nothing to do no reason to be.  Nobody wants me,

all I can do is ladle stew and I never ever get the chance!”

            “There was stew last week,” said Butts “And I heard Madge telling you how big

and beautiful you were and how she couldn’t do without you!”  In all fairness Madge said

that kind of thing to all the Crew.  Having grown up as an only child she had always been

in the habit of talking to inanimate objects and the Kitchen Crew was no exception, they

all basked in her admiration and praise.  Laddie blushed now as he remembered how

happy he had been when she had remarked on his handsome large bowl, comfy grip

handle and capacity for scooping up her homemade winter stew.

            “That’s the trouble though, isn’t it! The nicer she is to me the more I want to hear

and she just doesn’t make stew often enough.  Why, in summer I can sit in the drawer for

months on end!”

           “Well,” said Fillip, trying to get a word in, “You’re here now, come and be

useful!”

            “How?” said Laddie, “I can’t smell any stew.”  Fillip explained what he and Butts

suspected and how he, Fillip had hatched a daring plan to save Madge’s wallet. The plan

depended on large heavy objects and Laddie was one of them.  The other was Ted the

meat tenderiser and he was a different kettle of fish.

          Laddie wasn’t sure about the plan but was willing to help. Ted was not.

        “Yeh, yeh, yeh,” he said as he strutted around pretending to threaten everyone with

his massive, heavy knobbly head.  “Great plan, great plan, but count me out, I’m busy.”

           “What do you mean, busy,” said Butts indignantly.  “All you’re doing is strutting

around trying to look macho as usual!  There’s no steak tonight so you won’t be needed!”

           Ted paused, looking down on Butts and Fillip, he was nearly twice their size,

           “Listen here – midgets,” he said rudely.  “I am BUSY being threatening!  If

Madge wants to use me as a weapon to attack that thief then I’m ready and at her service,

all she has to do is pick me up and he’s mince!”

            Fillip sighed, “Are we talking about the same Madge here?” he asked. “Do you

really think she’s likely to attack anyone – with or without your help?  Why she even

opens the door and ushers flies outside!”

            “Well I’m ready anyway.” said Ted obstinately and went striding off to threaten

the electric kettle.

          “We need him,” said Butts, “We’ll just have to be crafty.  Let’s get the PLAN

ready.

          Laddie was persuaded to lie on the edge of the worktop in a particular place and

told he could take a nap if he liked.  Then Fillip and Butts watched and waited their

opportunity.

          Nick said to Madge pointing to the window behind her, “What is that building over

there?” Of course Madge turned to look and started to explain about the barn and her

sculptures.

           “This is it,” said Fillip “He’ll make his move in a minute.”

          Butts turned and called to Ted, “Hi Ted, come and look at Laddie, I’m a bit

worried about him.” Ted strutted across and looked down at the ladle lying there.

         “He  looks alright to me,” he said, “He’s just bored as usual.  He’s hardly ever used

while I’m used every week on the best steak!”  Ted stuck his nose in the air so he did not

see Fillip behind him.  Fillip bent down and Butts gave Ted a push.  The solid, top heavy

tenderiser staggered, fell backwards over Fillip and, taking Laddie with him, plunged off

the edge of the worktop.  At that very moment as Nick inched the wallet out of the bag

                                                                      34
while Madge’s back was turned there was a terrific, resounding crash as Ted and Laddie

hit Crumpet’s steel food bowl.  Madge whirled around just in time to see what Nick was

up to. Horrified she had no time to say anything before he was flying out of the door.

Madge was torn between grief at her lack of awareness of Nick’s character and relief that

she didn’t have to explain to Henry how she had lost the housekeeping money.

          She picked up Ted and Laddie and hugged them to her.  “Oh you clever, clever

boys!” she cried, if you hadn’t fallen off the worktop I wouldn’t have turned round to see

what the noise was!”

           “Fallen my foot!” said Ted aggrieved.  “We were pushed and very hard at that!”

“I might have lost one of my tenderiser knobs!”  But Laddie was delighted, “She said I

was a clever boy!” he murmured, “Oh I can live until the next stew on that!”

          Apart from Ted the only displeased person was Crumpet who, when called in for

dinner, found his dish had a dent in it.

           “How did that happen?” he spat at Fillip.

           “Don’t look at me,” said Fillip, “I didn’t do it, it was Ted.”  Crumpet looked up at

the fearsome Ted poised above him and decided to make the best of his dented dish and

the meat in it.

          When the hoo-ha had settled down Madge made herself a cup of tea and sat down

at the table.  Something was niggling at the edge of her mind.  She looked around the

kitchen.  She couldn’t say everything was in its place because from Saturday to Thursday,

0nothing was in its place.  But she felt something was wrong.  “Oh!” she grasped, “of

course there’s something missing – I had a parcel ready to post by the door and its gone!

                                                                    35
          The Kitchen Crew looked at each other anxiously.  They thought they had been so

clever and none of them had noticed Nick grabbing the parcel as he rushed out.  How

they wished they could comfort Madge.  But Madge didn’t seem to need comforting, she

was laughing so much tears were running down her cheeks. She felt she had to explain to

someone and there was only the Kitchen Crew around so she told them why she was

laughing, “You see darlings,” she giggled, “That parcel Nick stole contained a lot of old

clothes destined for Africa, I’d love to see him in them!”

title: Danger, Toddler About
body:
One of the peculiar things about the Kitchen Crew’s liveliness was its limitations.

Although they could sprout tiny arms and legs to move about the kitchen, they had very

little strength in them as they had no muscles. This was sometimes frustrating when they

couldn’t be as helpful as they would like but it was also useful as they could not fight

each other!  Their brief spats of ill humour would end with only a few feeble insults,

feeble because they all had their weaknesses and could have insults hurled right back.

However a big plus to their liveliness was that though usually inert when adults were

about they could take small liberties with children who had not yet learned to talk.  Many

a toddler visiting Madge’s kitchen had been enchanted to watch the Kitchen Crew

frolicking around but could only tell mummy, “A goo goo ducky coo.”  There had once

been an INCIDENT when a toddler had passed unnoticed from being unable to talk to

communication and had told his mum that his fork had winked at him!  Fortunately

mummy was the kind who heard what she expected to hear and saw only the sun glinting

on Florence’s face.  “Yes darling, isn’t it pretty,” she had cooed and everyone relaxed.

          The Kitchen Crew loved small children in their kitchen, they could show off  badly

and be rewarded with the smile and giggles they loved.  Then one day the limitation and

the plus came face to face.  Claude, the French Cheese Knife had been showing off,

something he did often. He was proud to be the only foreign utensil, Madge having

bought him back from a day trip to Calais.  He didn’t count Topsy the Corkscrew as

foreign because she only reflected what she had been used for (at the moment she was

Russian, Madge having opened a bottle of vodka mix with her, so she was going round

being dramatic and in comprehensible to everyone).

          Claude was also extremely proud of his very sharp point and liked to underline his

dual purpose. “You see, mes amis I cut ze cheese, and zut viola I pick it up aussi!”  So it

came as no surprise to the others when Claude in a fit of boastfulness, prancing around

too near the edge of the unit and bragging that because he was French and dual purpose

he was superior to the rest when he fell off and hit the ground, sharp point first.  The

kitchen flooring was a soft vinyl and Claude’s sharp bit stuck in very firmly, he was

helpless.  The others had no strength to pull him out and watched in horror as he juggled

around uselessly.

          “Oh well,” said Fillip, “He can’t do any harm while he’s held so firmly!”

WRONG!  The kitchen door opened slowly and a small body waddled into the room.

Jimmy was eighteen months old and could just about stagger around, However he could

not as yet put two words together coherently, as the Kitchen Crew knew, they relaxed –

momentarily.  Jimmy was drawn to the shiny waving thing on the floor and panic began.

          “No, no Jimmy!” shouted Fillip and Butts together.  Jimmy stopped confused

maybe they were more interesting?  Tentatively he stretched out a podgy fist towards

Claude.  Butts jumped in between them,

           “Hello Jimmy, are you going to play with me today?”

           “Goofle ga ga,” said Jimmy, which translated roughly as, “Not today thank you, I

have bigger fish to fry!”  And once again he reached towards Claude.  Fillip was frantic,

he always blamed himself for any trouble the Kitchen Crew got into as it was he who had

enlivened them.  For a moment he wished he had NOT enlivened Claude who would now

be sitting in a drawer and not causing trouble like this.  Even Claude was chastened

realising his boasting had been the reason for this problem.

           “Non, non, mon cher,” he said to Jimmy, “I am not good to play with n’es ce pan?

Find someone else s’il vous plait!”  But Jimmy was obstinate, he wanted that shiny

waving thing.

          Then just when Fillip and Butts were preparing to cover their eyes and wait for the

worst there came a reprieve. From the worktop near the sink came a great calamity,

clacking commotion.  The wooden spoon girls, so low down on the social scale of the

kitchen – because they were neither metal nor sharp, were about to save the day.

          Holly Wood, Olive Wood and Mable Maple were doing a clog dance.  Well they

didn’t actually have clogs of course but their wooden ends were being put to good use

and making a distraction by rattling up and down the counter top.  They actually got a

good rhythm going and then decided to liven it up with some wooden head bashing,

rather like some demented Morris Men.  Jimmy was enchanted, Claude forgotten for the

time being relaxed.  Staggering over to the wooden spoon gang he watched fascinated as

they cavorted and crashed their heads together for his benefit.  Sadly they were not used

to quite so much action being normally called up to stir some soup or beat a few eggs and

one by one they staggered back to their spoon box.  Only Mable Maple, the smallest was

left jigging around and even she finally collapsed in a straight faint.

          Jimmy got back to the business in hand, that shiny, sharp thing was still there.

Which was when Peter Pallet Knife came into his own.  Peter Pallet took a lot of semi

friendly abuse from the Kitchen Crew.

            “Call yourself a knife Peter?  What did you cut last then?  A dash maybe?”

           The Sharp Army were particularly brutal, “You’d like to join us wouldn’t you

Peter? We’re, awfully sorry, old boy but you don’t have what it takes, you know.  No

blade, no edge, no good for our Army, you’d be no good in a fight with the enemy.”

Which was a silly argument really as the Sharp Army never went anywhere to fight and

had no enemies. But Peter was demoralised and largely kept himself to himself, he wasn’t

used very often so unlike Butts Butterknife who was used everyday, he had a very low

opinion of himself.

          Now, however, he clambered down from his drawer and presented himself to

Jimmy.  He was twice the size of Claude and just as shiny (lack of use has its good

points) but with blunt edges, he couldn’t hurt anybody but he could bend and turn

amusingly.  Jimmy was interested and followed Peter as he edged the toddler away from

danger. Peter stopped in a patch of sunlight and twisting and turning to catch the rays of

light put on a dazzling show for the toddler.  Jimmy, in a trance, was totally occupied

with the entertainment. He watched gleefully, laughing his chubby laugh and clapping his

chubby hands for those vital few moments before Madge and his mum came into the

kitchen.  Peter fell to the floor, inert and innocent.  Madge exclaimed as she pulled

Claude from the vinyl and put him in the sink.

            “My,” she said, “What a good job Jimmy was playing with the flat blunt old pallet

knife and hadn’t spotted the cheese knife. He could have hurt himself badly with that.”

           The Kitchen Crew  sighed, they never got any credit for anything good they did!

But at least they had clear consciences and no blood on their blades.  There was another

advantage too; Claude was very quiet for the next few days.
.

title: Bonfire Night
body:
There was an unusual amount of activity in the kitchen.  Madge was to’ing and fro’ing

with bulging bags, filling the pantry, fridge and freezer with goodies.  The Kitchen Crew

was  trying to work out what was happening.

          “It’s a siege!” said General Carver knowingly.  “We’re going to be cut off by an

invading army and Madge and Henry are making sure they have enough food to last out.”

          “They’re going on holiday – self catering – crossing the Atlantic in an open boat!”

That was Sophie, the empty headed sieve.

          “It’s a party,” said Butts, “they’re going to have dozens and dozens of people in

and they need to be fed.  Don’t you remember, it happened last year too?”

          “Of course,” said Fillip,  “a Bonfire Party.”  They rushed to the French doors which

opened from the kitchen to the terrace and garden beyond.  “See – there’s Henry building

the Bonfire.”

          “Do you mean all those potatoes Madge bought are for baking, not putting into an

enormous stew?  “That was the sad voice of Laddie Ladle.  “Oh well, I expect they’ll use

me to pull the potatoes out of the fire,” he brightened at the thought of being useful after

all – even if he would get a bit scorched.

          The build up to Guy Fawkes Night continued and the Kitchen Crew were caught up

in it.  “It’ll be a chance to get out of the kitchen,” said Kitty Knife, “As long as we don’t

get LEFT outside," said her sister Knoeline. “Remember last year, Sid Spoon and Frances

Fork spent a night in the dew and got rained on, it’s a good job Henry keeps the grass

short.  They might NEVER HAVE BEEN FOUND!”

            “But they were!” said her more cheerful and optimistic brother Ken.  “Don’t be

such a misery  Knoeline. They always remember us – eventually!”

           November 5th dawned cold, bright and clear.  Madge was excited. She had had a

wonderful idea for a theme party.  The guests were to wear fancy dress and the garden

would be adorned with some of her lighter sculptures.

          The guests started to arrive and the Kitchen Crew took bets on who was wearing

the most unusual costume.

          Between those who knew Madge’s work were a lot  of Straw Man and the Tin Man

from the Wizard of Oz.  While Henry’s friends tended to come as Bean Brummel and

Albert Einstein.  A well worn path was developing between the kitchen and the garden as

food was ferried out and dirty plates brought back.  Many of the Kitchen Crew were

pressed into service.  Laddie was overjoyed to be employed as baked potato retriever

from the blazing bonfire. Topsy the Corkscrew/bottle Opener was incoherent as she was

used to open wine and beer and quite lost track of which accent she should be using. The

wooden spoon girls huddled together and tried to be ignored, terrified that Madge would

use them to dig around in the fire.  Ted Tenderiser lay quietly in his drawer, totally tired

out  from tenderising steak all afternoon. Butts was dizzy from the number of rolls he had

buttered. The tong twins – Anna and Belle were wild with excitement as they were

pressed into picking up sausages and salad.

           “Dual purpose at last,” whispered Anna, “Wait till Claude hears about US doing

two things!”

           “Well they’re not really two different things!” said Belle, as awkward as ever.

Although joined together they rarely agreed on anything and had to be pressed together

quite firmly to do their job.

           “So you don’t think sausage and salad are two different things eh?  Well I beg to

differ!”

           “Don’t be stupid Anna, you know what I mean!  We pick them up, but don’t do

anything else!  Not like clever old Claude anyway!”

           “Just because he talks to you, you think he’s wonderful!”

           “You’re just jealous because he talks to me and not to you!”

           “Don’t be silly – if he talks to one he talks to both of us!”

           “Well I think he’s looking at me when he talks!”

           “Yes, I thought he was a bit cross eyed!”  This effectively shut Belle up and they

were plunged once more into the salad bowl.  The knife and fork family were in their

element – being only sets of six since Henry’s purge they had been joined by several

plastic additions but Fillip was careful not to enliven them as they wouldn’t be around

after the party. He also winced when one of them was snapped by too much pressure and

was VERY GLAD he hadn’t enlivened them and made friends.

          The best part came at 10pm when Henry unveiled the FIREWORKS. Actually

there were several unveilings because Henry and Madge had, for once, worked in perfect

harmony.  Madge’s sculptures had been set around the garden and before being covered

in old curtains and other bits and pieces Henry had carefully wedged his fireworks in

between the various parts of each sculpture.  As each was uncovered, he lit the firework

and there were gasps of awe and delight from the guests.  Madge was in heaven, since

she thought the gasps were admiration for her sculpture and missed the odd comment

about it being strange to set up the fireworks amongst all that old scrap metal.

          “Darling,” said one friend to her, “What a brilliant idea to light up the garden like

that!” Madge simpered, “Yes, that one took a month to build you know!” My, thought the

friend fancy planting those fireworks a month ago, what foresight and planning!  "And,”

Madge rushed on, "they’re for sale afterwards!” “Oh Madge, you are funny!  I don’t think

you’ll find a market for used gunpowder!”  Madge laughed, “Oh that’s just one thing I

used! One of them is made of knives and forks!”  The friend gave Madge a sideways look

and edged away nervously, Madge was becoming really strange.  Maybe it was time to

end the friendship in case madness was catching!

          Gradually as the bonfire died down the guests took their leave and the garden

became quiet.  Of course some of the Kitchen Crew were left outside – that was

inevitable in this household!  But they weren’t worried, Henry would be around to clean

up in the morning if not before and he would pick up any item not taken back to the

kitchen.

         Among the abandoned cutlery were Fillip, Butts and Sophie Sieve (no one knew

just who had taken her into the garden and she was a bit vague about her duties – “Oh

you know I just picked things up!”  Maybe there had been too much wine flowing?

When all was quiet they wandered around to see what Henry would have to deal with in

the morning.

          “Just look – potato skins in the trees!”

          “And salad in the fishpond!”

          “At least Crumpet will enjoy those sausages someone left in the bird bath!

           Amongst the debris were many spent fireworks, already getting damp with dew.

Then Butts made an alarming find.

           “Look!” he said, grabbing Fillip’s arms and pointing. At the edge of a flower-bed

half hidden by a large dahlia was a very big firework.  Unlike the others it was not spent,

unlike the others it was not getting damp and unlike the others it was stuck in the soil,

angled and pointing at the house.  Its blue paper smouldered slowly.

          Fillip and Butts were horrified.  “If it goes off it will head for Madge and Henry’s

bedroom window – the one they always leave open, it could set fire to the curtains “Fillip

was frantic. “What on earth can we do?”

          Sophie was no help, “Don’t ask me!” she said, “you know my head is full of

holes!”

          Butts said, “We have to make sure Henry notices it!”

          “I have an idea,” said Fillip.  I think there are other members of the Crew here,

quickly let’s round them up. “ After five minutes they had found some spoons, knives

and forks and even General Carver, a bit put out at having been separated from his staff.

          Fillip explained, “You know Henry,” he began “He always checks around and

while he would miss some of us in the dark he will pick up all he can see …so …” Fillip

told them his plan.  It was a daring plan, one which might cause endless trouble if Henry

really thought about it, they just hoped he wouldn’t.

          The front door closed on the last guest.  The bedroom light went on, the Kitchen

Crew waited expectantly.  Fillip knew Henry so well…

         ” I’ll just pop out to the garden Madge and see if there’s anything left out.”

         Henry took a torch from the drawer and made his way down the garden.      “Oh

dear!” he exclaimed.  “Who brought the sieve out side?”  He bent down and picked

Sophie up.  His eye was drawn to Laddie who was just beyond.  As he picked up the ladle

he saw a few inches further on a fork and beyond it a knife.  Henry began to think it a

little strange that they seemed to be in an almost straight line and were leading him on!

But Henry was tired and a little fuddled with wine and that cosy feeling one has when

one has thrown a REALLY good party.  Carefully he picked up a spoon and another fork,

being drawn all the while to the last flower bed.  And then he saw it, the smouldering

rocket!  “How fortunate I came out to tidy up!”  He exclaimed. “Who knows what might

have happened if that had gone off?”  Fillip sighed, he knew who knew, but once again

there was no praise for the Kitchen Crew!

title: Crumpet foiled!
body:
Life in Madge and Henry’s kitchen lumbered on from Madge’s untidiness during most of

the week to Henry’s Friday night tidying binges.

       It was just as well that Henry was an accountant, thought Fillip; he not only put

things away but also counted everything to make sure all were present and correct.  This

was necessary from the Kitchen Crew’s point of view because they had an enemy.

Crumpet the cat was aware of the Kitchen crew’s liveliness and he didn’t approve.

Before Fillip arrived and enlivened all and sundry Crumpet knew where he was.  If he did

something wrong, like stealing some food not meant for him he was punished (usually by

being put outside for five minutes – Madge was forgiving as well as forgetful).  This he

accepted as part of life’s rich pattern.  It didn’t happen very often, as Crumpet was quite

intelligent. He stole only small bits of food that were not noticed and he tried to make

sure that if it was a BIG, NOTICEABLE bit of food the weather outside the back door

was not too cold or wet in case he was thrown out.

          However, with the Kitchen Crew able to move around Crumpet never knew from

one minute to the next what he might he blamed for!  There was the noise in the night

when Fillip and Butts had rescued Topsy by breaking a bottle of wine – that was a cold,

wet night to be thrown out as Crumpet remembered all too well.  Neither had he forgotten

the DISH DENTING time when Ted and Laddie had crashed into his food bowl to alert

Madge to a thief.  Crumpet cared nothing for the reasons behind these events, he was a

cat and the universe turned around him.  He had no loyalty, no finer feelings, and no

desire to help anyone else. He just WAS, and very attractively too with wonderful black

and grey stripes, a long sleek  tail and a lofty expression. One of his favourite games was

to play with any helpless member of the Kitchen Crew which Madge had left on the floor

and finish the game by batting the poor unfortunate under the kitchen cooker.  Since

Madge was a careless housekeeper there could be quite a little gathering underneath by

the time Henry arrived to tidy up.
