       It was a Friday morning and Fillip had been under the cooker for two days, Madge

hadn’t missed him, there were other forks she could use!  As Fillip dozed he heard

Crumpet push his way through the cat flap.

      “I wonder if he’s going to knock anyone else under here?” mused Fillip.  “I could do

with some company, I’m bored.”

       He heard Crumpet’s little patterings and scratchings as he played with something

then without warning there was a furry rush and Fillip was pushed even further under the

cooker.  He couldn’t leap to his little feet, there was no room but he rolled over and came

face to face with a pair of red rimmed pink eyes!  It was a mouse. Crumpet had caught a

field mouse and brought it home to play with before eating it as a starter to his main

meal.  Now, annoyed, he tried to get his plaything back.  First one then another furry arm,

claws stretched out, tried to hook the hapless mouse.  Crumpet was furious, he had been

working up an appetite for that mouse.  He could have got rid of it, bones and all, and no

one would have been any the wiser.  Now if Madge or Henry thought they had mice he

would be accused of being a useless hunter and threatened with being replaced by a

mousetrap that didn’t need feeding. Crumpet played on his supposed usefulness as a

mouser by sometimes leaving a dead strew on the doorstep.   He liked the praise and

extra titbits he was rewarded with and he didn’t like the taste of shrews anyway.  Mice

were different, plump and juicy, he had been looking forward to this one.

       As flat as he could get, Crumpet peered under the cooker and saw the mouse and

Fillip huddled at the back.

     “Hey Fillip, do me a favour,” Crumpet was very pleasant.  “Just chase that mouse out

for me, there’s a good fellow!”

     Fillip looked back, a neutral look on his face,

    “Do you mean you want me to help you Crumpet?  Like you helped Butts and me to

rescue Topsy?” Crumpet spat rudely and withdrew for a wash and a think.

     The mouse was trembling with fright,

    “Thank you, sir!” it said, “I don’t know what I was thinking of to allow myself to be

caught by that lazy, fat lump of lard  – I haven’t been well you know!”

   “You’ll be even less well if you move out of here at the moment.  If you wait until

Crumpet has gone I’ll see what I can do to help you.”

    “Thank you, sir” said the mouse humbly, “anything you can do will be greatly

appreciated, I’m sure.”

     Fillip was wracking his brains without much success.  The only way out to the garden

was through the door or the cat flap.  Both were much too heavy for any of the Kitchen

Crew to open.  They would have to wait for Madge to open the door and then have the

mouse run past her but there was an awfully big space of kitchen floor to cross and If

Madge, who was afraid of mice, saw it she would not only scream the house down but

throw things at it too.  The things thrown would naturally be members of the Kitchen

Crew who might get dented or even broken.

     Butts ambled over, peering under the cooker he saw there was a visitor in the kitchen.

Fillip told him that the mouse needed help.

     “We’ll have to do something soon,” he said, “It’s Friday and Henry will be in to sort

us all out, he’s bound to see the mouse when he looks for you Fillip!”  Fillip groaned,

“I’d forgotten about that,” he said, “We don’t have much time!”

     Just then Madge breezed in, “I must hang the washing out before Henry takes over,”

she hummed as she worked.  This time it was Butts who had the GRAND IDEA!

     “Look!” he said, “over there, on the floor is Sophie Sieve – if we can get the mouse

under her he can make his way towards the door and rush out when Madge takes the

washing out – but we don’t have much time!”

     At that moment the telephone rang in the hall and Madge went to answer it.

     “Now!” said Fillip to the mouse “Quick, follow us.”  They pattered across the floor to

where Sophie was lying.

    “Sophie, we need your help!  Do you want to be involved in a GRAND RESCUE?”

    “Oh yes, please, anything for a laugh, a long as you don’t need me to hold water or

anything difficult like that!”

8     Fillip explained and helped the mouse crawl under Sophie’s big dome.  Slowly and

awkwardly the mouse and Sophie shuffled across the room to the back door, urged on by

Fillip and Butts.  At last they were as close as they could get and tired out and breathless.

Sophie and the mouse waited. At last  Madge came back and carried on getting the

washing ready to go out. There was a heart stopping moment when Madge looked out

and wondered if it was going to rain.   But she decided to risk it and opened the door.

Looking around she spotted Sophie and with her foot moved the sieve to hold the door

open!  Fillip let out his breath with a sigh and whispered to Butts,

      ” I really think Madge is on our side!”   As Madge disappeared down the garden with

an armful of clothes, Sophie tipped herself over and the mouse crept out.

       “Thank you, thank you!” he murmured tearfully – if there’s ever anything I can do –

let me know.”  With a parting wave he scampered out to the  safety of the shrubbery.

The Kitchen Crew relaxed and congratulated each other on another job well done.

Crumpet on the other hand, when he came back from his siesta in the study to find his tit

bit had escaped, thought dark thoughts and promised revenge on the Kitchen Crew.

title: Fiona and the Whisk
body:
Madge put the phone down and called out to Henry in great excitement.  “Henry, Henry –

What do you think?  Fiona is coming to visit!  It’s such a long time since she was here!”

      It was, and Henry knew why, but neither he nor Fiona would dream of telling Madge!

Strangely just as Trevor seemed to take after his stepmother, Fiona was a young Henry.

She was careful, exact and above all very, very neat.  She loved her mother and stepfather

dearly and kept in regular contact by telephone, cards,  letters and e-mail.  She often

arranged to meet Madge in London for a day visit but she avoided the family home as

much as possible.  The truth was that somewhere along the line Madge and Fiona had

somehow swapped roles.  While she was growing up Madge had been a model mother in

many respects.  She had encouraged her daughter and stepson to be responsible, think for

themselves and always put others first. One thing she had never insisted upon, unlike

most mothers, was that they tidy their rooms!  Madge did not believe in double standards,

SHE was untidy, how could she ask her children to be otherwise!  Besides their rooms

were the only little space in the whole world which belonged just to them.  She would

hint that if they did tidy up they would not always be searching for things!  “Don’t end up

like me!” She would laugh nervously – aware that she was not a very good example.  The

end result was that Trevor when he moved into his own flat carried on as before and was

in a constant state of chaos which often made him late for appointments.  Fiona on the

other hand became a demon neatness fanatic. Her style was simple and she enjoyed the

extra space she could get into her small flat by putting everything away.  Once her

mother had visited and asked thoughtlessly if Fiona had been burgled, as there didn’t

seem to be much left. Fiona was amused.  “No mother, this is ME!  This is how I like to

live!”  Madge was baffled.  How could anyone live comfortably with no ornaments or

knick-knacks or holiday reminders about? In the tiny kitchenette the work surfaces

gleamed and stretched emptily around.  Madge didn’t know whether to admire or

deplore.  She decided to be diplomatic. “Its lovely Fee, so ------ ready for you to leave

your own personal mark on it” Fiona sighed.  “I have mother and this is my mark!”

Madge bemused changed the subject.

            Now as Madge’s excitement at the forthcoming visit died down she looked

critically around her own kitchen and saw it for the first time through Fiona’s eyes.

                 “Oh dear,” said Madge, “Where shall I start?”

So it was that the Kitchen Crew found they had a sort of Friday night in the middle of the

week. It was only ‘sort of’ because Madge did not tidy up the way Henry did.  To Henry

everything had its own place and should be in it.  Madge felt glad if things were just out

of sight. Luckily   there were no carpets or rugs in the kitchen or some things might have

found themselves out of sight and out of mind for a long time.

            The kitchen Crew was interested, they hadn’t met Fiona since they had been

enlivened by Fillip Fork but they had picked up many clues from bits of conversation

between Madge and Henry.

            “I went shopping with Fiona today – do you know, she didn’t buy a thing!”

(Madge).

            “I’ve got a very good computer programme for Fee about financial

developments.”  (Henry).

            “Fee says I should arrange the house according to Feng Shui, have you heard of

him?” (Madge).

“Fee has given me her holiday dates for the next five years, I must put them in my diary.”

(Henry).

          Fiona arrived.  She had prepared herself for the disorder she remembered from her

years at home so at first she was pleasantly surprised, on entering the kitchen to find it

looking relatively tidy.

          “Shall I make us a cup of coffee?” she asked Madge.

          “Oh yes darling, you know where everything is.”

          Which wasn’t the same as where everything was supposed to be.  So it took Fiona

a while to find mugs (in the larder) teaspoons cuddling up in the wooden spoon box and

coffee pretending to be sugar.  Gradually Fiona realised not much had changed, her

mother had simply become craftier at covering up.

          When they were settled at the kitchen table with their coffee and a tin of cream

crackers (well the packet looked very like the custard creams Madge had meant to buy).

Fiona produced a box and gave it to her mother.  “I bought this for you when I was in the

States last month.  You’re very difficult to buy for but I didn’t think you had one of these.

It will save your bowls getting scrape marks from the forks you’ve been using.”

            The Kitchen Crew listened anxiously, it sounded as though the fork family might

be made redundant.  Out of the box Madge pulled a large stainless steel egg whisk and

she was pleased, not thrilled exactly, but pleased that Fiona had put some thought into a

present for her – even if it was a bit –homely.

          “It’s very smart, Fee! And American too  - I’ll have to introduce it to the cheese

knife – it’s French!”  The visit went very well.  Fiona did a little secret tidying  of utensils

to their various drawers and boxes.  It gave her a sense of satisfaction and Madge didn’t

mind because she didn’t notice, she was used to opening several drawers before she

found what she wanted.

          After Fiona left the kitchen was quiet.  The Kitchen Crew waited expectantly but

the new whisk had nothing to say.  “Of course!” said Fillip to himself, “It’s down to me

to enliven her.”

          He went across to the place where Madge had put the whisk  until she found a

fitting place for it. Fillip applied his right hand and left foot and stood back. The whisk

stirred and shuddered and sat up.  It looked around with interest then spotted Fillip

          “Well hi there sonny, how d’ya do?”  I’m Wilma,  the whisk, what’s cooking?”

          Fillip sniffed the air, “Eh nothing! Madge and Henry have gone to bed!”  The

whisk laughed loudly

          “Gee, you’re a funny little guy but then I guess you are a Limey.”  Fillip was

flummoxed,

           “Eh no actually I’m a fork!  Haven’t you met a fork before?

           Wilma looked at him smiling, “Yes, honey, I’ve met a fork but I guess you haven’t

met an American whisk before!”   She sighed, “Now I know what they were talking

about in the hardware store when they said Yanks and Brits were two nations divided by

a common tongue!  I guess we’re going to have to learn each other’s language!  Now,

come on honey, introduce me to the other members here!”

.           Fillip took her to meet the Sharp Army,

           “Pleased to meet you ma’am,”

           “Well look at you lot, aren’t you the bees’ knees!”

            General Carver looked annoyed, he wasn’t sure if he’d been insulted.  Captain

Bread nudged him,

            “It’s OK Sir, bees knees are good!” (He had once had to cut up some bagels and

knew a little American).

             The wooden spoons were impressed and giggled a lot, Wilma smiled kindly and

moved on.  The table cutlery were introduced and made her welcome.  Ted Tenderizer

was gruffly polite and Laddie Ladle briefly put aside thoughts of Irish stew as opposed to

Lancashire hotpot to offer his services should he be needed.  Surprisingly, it was Peter

Pallett who caused Wilma most interest.

            “Well, look at you!  Aren’t you dashing, tall and slim, I like that in a guy!”  Peter

was speechless, no one had ever commended him for his appearance before and he was

overcome, he could only splutter.  Wilma laughed,

             “Don’t be shy, big guy,” she murmured, “You and I have a lot in common, we

must get together sometime!” and she winked at him.

In her wisdom Madge had decided to put Wilma in with the wooden spoons.  Wilma

didn’t mind, she was not proud or vain and would hob nob with anyone.  After an initial

spate of giggling the wooden spoon girls made her welcome.

              “I’m glad you’re not sharp!” (Holly)

              “Wood you like a bit more room dear, I’ll move up.”

                “Ooer – you are tall!” (little Mable Maple).

                 It must have been fate that made Madge stuff Peter, head down into the

wooden spoon box, next to Wilma.  They got on like a house on fire, (an unfortunate

simile since they shared their resting place with the wooden spoons!)

               Various members of the Crew were trying to work out how Wilma’s arrival

would affect them.

              “Fiona was right in a way,” said Fergus, “I’ve never felt right beating up eggs,

the sound of my prongs scraping on the bottom of the bowl gives me a headache, so

although there’ll be less work I won’t miss that awful noise.”

              “It was hard work beating pancake batter,” said Olive  “Madge was never

satisfied with the lightness and I felt I’d let her down!”

            “Madge used me to whip cream once,” said Flossie fork “She went on so long I

thought I’d ‘ave an ‘eart attack!”

              No one could think of any bad result from Wilma’s arrival so they were all more

inclined to welcome her to the Kitchen Crew.  She was foreign after all and a bit exotic

though she didn’t seem to be the sort to parade it like Claude Cheese Knife.  He had been

very quiet while the debate about Wilma’s usefulness had gone on, now he spoke

sneeringly,

              “Huh, she ‘as only ze one function, I think, she whisks – nothing else – whereas

I – I am dual purpose – I really earn my keep!”

              “Oh, shut up Claude, at least she’s not big headed like you!”  The chorus was

general and Claude retired in a Gallic fit of the sulks.  Since you can’t talk while you sulk

everyone was quite glad he felt insulted!

                 So Wilma the Whisk settled in happily, accepted by most of the crew and

waited for her moment of glory to come

title: Fire! Fire!
body:
Madge was delightful, charming and feckless. It wasn’t that she was silly, she just didn’t

think much about what she was doing, she might be thinking of something that had

happened yesterday or she might be planning for tomorrow but generally the “here and

now” got little attention. Luckily for Madge she very seldom had to face the results of her

mindlessness because fate seemed to smile fondly upon her.

            Like the time she went to bed and left the back door unlocked – something Henry

was very careful about, “You never know who’s about these days!” he would solemnly

say to Madge, “so I not only lock the door but take the key out and place it where it can’t

be reached by someone breaking a window.”

            “You think of everything Henry,” said the admiring Madge.  “I just wouldn’t

expect anyone to break in here.”  “We don’t have anything work stealing!”  “Just as

well,” she thought, “since I left it open last night!”

            Henry was patient, “The thieves wouldn’t know that until the damage had been

done,” he explained, “and then they might just trash the place out of spite!”

            Inwardly Henry thought that if they broke into Madge’s kitchen they might think

someone had already been in and made as much mess as possible before leaving empty

handed.  Madge had never told Henry about Nick who had been a thief and had actually

been invited in!  She had been careful since to keep any unknown callers on the doorstep.

She had even kept the new vicar outside in the rain until he produced identification and

proof he had not brought his dog collar from Oxfam!

            But many disasters start in the home and owe nothing to outside evil influences.

            Very occasionally Madge cooked chips.  She liked the taste but not the

responsibility of watching them all the time and she didn’t like cleaning up the mess from

the oily splatters which resulted when the heat was too high.  Henry didn’t like the

greasiness when the heat was too low. Since getting the heat just right was a fiddly thing

Madge did baked potatoes, boiled potatoes, mashed potatoes and occasionally oven chips

but genuine home made chips she only did when Trevor was expected as they were his

favourites.

            Trevor was expected so Madge prepared to concentrate and make some beautiful

home made chips upon which she would be congratulated by Trevor and Henry.  Trevor

and Henry went to the pub for a drink before lunch and Madge concentrated really hard.

Just enough heat, not too much, not too little, the oil simmered gently.  And then the

‘phone rang.  It made Madge jump because she had just been wondering when Fiona

would ring next and there she was! Madge wondered briefly if perhaps she had ESP as

she hurried to answer the phone in the hall.  The back door was open and the breeze

slammed the door to the hall closed.  Madge could not hear the sizzling pan only Fiona

on the phone.  Fiona with news to tell.

            For a while nobody in the kitchen thought anything amiss.  Then Wilma the

Whisk began to stir uneasily.  From her position in the wooden spoon box on the shelf

next to the cooker she watched as the oil crept slowly up the inside of the fryer.

           “Say, is that not just a teeny bit dangerous?” she said to Peter Pallett next to her.

           “I don’t know,” said Peter “I don’t remember Madge cooking chips before.

Why?”

           “Well,” said Wilma “if that pan boils over onto the gas there could be a fire!”  The

wooden spoon girls shuddered in horror.

           “I saw it once on TV in the States.  The whole kitchen was in flames in minutes!”

            Nobody knew what to say.

            Then Fillip ventured,

           “We can’t do anything about it!  We are not strong enough to turn the cooker off,

let alone move the pan.”

          “We’re doomed, doomed!” wailed Larry Ladle, “we’re metal, we’ll melt in the

heat.  The house will burn down and Henry and Madge will be cinders!”

          “Oh hey, sonny, don’t be such a misery, maybe we can do something together!”

          ”What?” chorused the kitchen Crew eagerness and anxiety mixed.

          “Well,” began Wilma “I seem to remember seeing another program– there was a

TV in the hardware store where I lived in the States – about what to do if there was a fire

in a pan – but I can’t quite recall ……” She racked her brain as she tried to remember

what the fireman in the yellow hard hat had said.

          “Us spoons could get water and put it out?” said Sam Spoon, very proud of his

ingenuity.

          “Heavens no!” exclaimed Wilma “I know that’s wrong – water makes it worse!”

          There was silence, no one else had any ideas.

          “Got it, got it, got it,” she suddenly exclaimed. “All we have to do is put a damp

cloth over it

           The others looked at her blankly.

            “But how do you propose we do that?” asked Fillip, exasperated. “We have

hardly any strength!”

         “But,” said Wilma getting quite excited now, “if we call work together we might

have just enough strength to do it – but you’ll all have to co-operate – all of you” she said

looking pointedly at Claude.

           Butts was excited, “There are some T-cloths soaking in the sink!” he said

           “No, no!” cried Wilma, “They’re much too wet – it would be like pouring water

on!”

            Silence.  Butts looked around in desperation.  “Down there!” he said “Madge

wrung out those T-cloths recently and hung them on the airer, they must be pretty damp

still, but not really wet.”

          “How much time do we have?” asked Fillip of Wilma who had now become the

expert on fires.

           Wilma looked at the pan,

           “Not much” she said “Maybe five minutes – we’ll have our work cut out to drag a

T-cloth up here and over the pan, lets get started!”

            As many of the Kitchen Crew who were not shut in drawers got down on to the

floor and raced across to the clothes airer.  Fortunately the cloth on the bottom rung was

still quite damp. Tiny hands seized an edge and started dragging it across the floor.

            The dampness made the cloth quite heavy and they were soon exhausted.

           “Next group,” called Wilma who had stayed on the counter to direct operations.

            The second group of the Kitchen Crew took over the dragging and got the cloth as

far as the feet of the cooker, then they too stopped, gasping.

           “Next” called Wilma

             Some of the bigger tools came forward and laboriously started, not only dragging

now but climbing up the drawer handles – their usual method of getting up and down

from floor to counter.

            It was so much harder with that heavy damp cloth to pull but eventually they

made it and they too collapsed from exhaustion.

          “Now” said Wilma looking at Peter Pallet, its up to you and me Peter, we’re the

tallest and only two can do this bit.  Are you ready?  Are you up to it?  You do have a

wooden handle – are you brave enough to take the risk?”

            Peter looked at Wilma and felt he could do anything for the Whisk who had made

him feel so good.  Solemnly he said,

           “For you Wilma, and Madge - anything!”

            Together they took a corner of the now grubby T-cloth and made their way slowly

the last few inches to the boiling pan.  Just as they got there the pan boiled over and

flames shot up into the air.

           “Now Peter, its now or never!” cried Wilma and together they raced forward one

on either side of the pan and dragged the cloth over the top. The flames were doused and

the smell of singed cloth filled the air, it filtered under the door and alerted Madge.  She

rushed in and put out the gas.  Everyone relaxed.  Then tensed again.  Was their secret

going to be discovered?  How would Madge explain how the cloth over the pan had got

there?  They need not have worried.  It was sometime later before Madge even began to

wonder at the fortunate turn of events and how lucky she was not to have to explain to

Henry why he had come home to a pile of ashes. She looked at the badly  burned cloth

and her forehead puckered.  She looked up above the stove at the clothes line she

sometimes used to dry things quickly.

          “I must be losing my marbles!” she said.  “I thought I’d put all the clothes on the

airer!  What a good job I must have put one up there and it fell at just the right time and

in just the right place!”

           Later Wilma looked at Peter and shrugged in disbelief,

          “That is one gullible lady!” she laughed.  “It’s a pity we can’t get the credit for our

hard work, but I suppose its just as well she doesn’t know what goes on here!”

Outside the door Madge paused and smiled a secret smile.

title: Bad Eggs
body:
Surprisingly for one so scatty, Madge was a careful wiper and mopper of work surfaces

and floors.  She loved clean shining surfaces though she wasn’t too bothered about

cluttering them up as soon as she had cleaned them.The fridge was often emptied,

cleaned and restocked, but Madge seemed to have a blind spot where sell by dates were

concerned.  Early in their marriage before Henry realised that he had to do the logical

thinking for them both he had airily remarked to Madge that sell by dates were just a

guide, and didn’t have to be taken too seriously as the manufacturer usually left a wide

margin for error.  As a result Madge had long since stopped looking at them.  Shopping

took long enough anyway because she invariably lost her shopping list and had to

examine every section carefully while picturing the kitchen and mentally filling the gaps.

This would have worked pretty well for most people, but Madge was not most people.

Madge was a one off – hopefully, the world would collapse in on itself if there had been

too many like her.  So where Madge’s shopping plan fell down was because, as with the

Kitchen Crew, she never put things in the same place twice.  So as she visualised the gap

on the third shelf down in the pantry she distinctly saw it was empty whereas last week it

had had a large box of cornflakes on it.  So a large box of cornflakes went into the trolley,

later to be placed in the pantry next to the other large box of cornflakes which had been

on the top shelf for the last three days. Which was why Madge was heading for a

calamity.

            Madge added a box of 6 large eggs to her trolley and planned a soufflé for dinner.

Once home she put all her groceries away, very pleased that she had remembered to put

wine in the fridge (not the freezer) and frozen beefburgers in the freezer (not the fridge).

The eggs went into the fridge with a box of eggs already there.

            “Funny,” thought Madge, “I forgot I had eggs – well never mind they last for ages

anyway.  “She turned aside as a sneeze overwhelmed her and blew her nose for the

umpteenth time that day. Madge was coping witha massive cold but refused to give in.

So far so good, all the groceries were away so it looked like a straightforward evening

meal of soufflé and salad.  Except for one thing.  Madge wasn’t very good at knowing her

left from her right!  Every time she cleared out the fridge when she found she had two

boxes of eggs she would put them to one side and say to herself, “left first, right second”

but when it came to replacing them this quite often became “right first, left second.”  You

would expect this to even out eventually, or not to matter very much but in fact for the

2last year Madge had been getting her egg boxes the wrong way round and every time the

oldest box had been put to the back of the fridge for later use.  Since Madge never looked

at ‘use-by’ dates anymore she now failed to notice that the eggs put out for use in the

soufflé were now more than ONE YEAR OUT OF DATE!

            Shopping sorted, Madge turned her attention to the soufflé. Sniffing and blowing

alternately she coped stoically with her cold feeling that Henry would appreciate how

long suffering she was and how wonderful to cope with a cold and the cooking.

She broke the eggs into a bowl.  The nearest members of the Kitchen Crew who

happened to be Fillip, Butts and Wilma were awakened from a light doze by a dreadful

smell!  Madge, completely stuffed up with her cold did not notice.

          “Pooh, what a stink!” said Wilma, “I hope she’s not planning to whip up those bad

eggs with me, I’ll be ill for a week!”

            “I’m afraid that’s just what she is going to do,” said Butts sounding worried.

Obviously her cold is stopping her from smelling anything!”

            “Well I’m not sticking around!” said Wilma and moved off the worktop when

Madge’s back was turned.  She bounced gently on the floor and rolled as far under the

cooker as she could.

            “Oh come on!” said Madge with unusual bad humour, “Why did you do that?

The Kitchen Crew were not worried, Madge wasn’t really talking to them and certainly

didn’t expect a reply.  Spending a lot of time on her own she often indulged in one sided

conversations, probably because she was an only child and had talked to her dolls and

teddy bear, when little.

            Madge added some grated cheese then suddenly noticed it had started to rain.

          “My washing!” she cried and disappeared out of the back door to rescue her

laundry.

           “Come on!” said Fillip, “We’ve got to think of something quickly before Madge

comes back to finish the cooking.”

           “Got it!” shouted Butts rather pleased that for once he had thought of something

clever before Fillip.  “We’ll need Crumpet to help, he’ll be in for his meal any minute

now.”

           “You must be joking if you think Crumpet is going to help us – especially after we

helped his supper to escape!”   Fillip was not hopeful.

            “Ah!” said Butts, “he won’t actually know he’s helping until it’s too late!”

            Breathlessly he described to Fillip how his plan would work.

           “First” he said, “We need that furry mitten of Madge’s taken up to the shelf up

there.” He pointed to a shelf above the bowl of eggs.  There was only one thing on it, a

fairly heavy saucepan, quite large and really too big for the narrow shelf.

           “We can’t shift that pan,” said Fillip beginning to understand Butts plan, “it’s

much too heavy for us!”

            “Don’t worry about that,” said Butts, “ just help me drag that mitten up there.

Then we’ll need one of the teaspoons to help – ‘cos they’re quite small.”

            “OK”, said Fillip “Tommy Teaspoon – come out and help us!”  Tommy climbed

out of the drawer with difficulty, he was really very small but very proud to be included

in a rescue plan.

            “Yes sir, please sir. What do you want me to do?”

            “See that furry mitten Butts and I just dragged up to that shelf?  We want you to

climb in side and wiggle when we tell you to!

              At that moment the cat flap clanged and Crumpet arrived for his meal. There

was nothing in his dish yet and he wasn’t very pleased.  He looked around for someone to

blame and spotted Fillip staring up at the shelf.

             “What are you doing?” he asked the fork.

              “Just looking,” said Fillip mysteriously and continued to crane his head as

though there was something really fascinating going on up there.”

               “Eh …… that mouse you lost the other day – what colour was it?”  Crumpet

was immediately interested,

             “Brown” he said “Why?”

            “Oh nothing,” said Fillip airily, “I just thought I saw something brown and furry

moving up on that shelf!”

              Crumpet looked up suspiciously and caught sight of the furry mitten just

twitching behind the pan.  He didn’t hear Butts whispered instructions to Tommy to

wiggle.

              Without a second thought Crumpet leapt with the fluid movement from floor to

counter top to shelf.  Just the other side of the pan he saw another small movement as

Tommy followed his whispered instructions.  Crumpet put out a paw, then pushed his

nose between pan and wall, he was almost in reach of the delicious brown, furry morsel.

A push too far and then was a huge crash.   Crumpet watched appalled as the bowl of

eggs broke under the weight of the heavy pan.  This was going to cost him more than five

minutes in the garden and at dinner time too!  He turned on Fillip but he was long gone

and Butts was nowhere to be seen either.  The furry mitten as Crumpet could now see

lying lifelessly upon the shelf, Tommy Teaspoon was back in his drawer reciting his

adventures and how he had assisted the boss to his envious brothers and sisters.

Madge returned with her washing in time to see Crumpet disappear through his cat flap –

why wait to be thrown out in?”

                 “That cat!” she exclaimed “is getting beyond a joke – I don’t know what’s got

into him lately!”  She cleared up the mess and put it into the waste bin.  Now she had to

start dinner all over again.

                    At that moment Henry walked into the kitchen.  After giving Madge a kiss

he wrinkled his nose.

             “What is that awful smell darling?” he asked.

             ”What smell?” said Madge – “I can’t smell anything – in fact I can’t smell

ANYTHING!  I’ve got a cold.”

               Henry opened the waste bin – “What happened here?” he asked it smells like a

school chemistry lab!”

               Madge told him what Crumpet had done.  Henry looked deeper into the bin and

pulled out the egg carton.  He pointed out the date to Madge.

           “It looks like Crumpet did us a favour!” he said.  “You’d better give him an extra

helping of cat food tonight!”

                                                                          69

title: Flood, all hands on deck
body:
Madge was in a bad mood, fortunately this didn’t happen often as it gave many of the

Kitchen Crew headaches.  They knew she was in a bad mood as soon as she started to

wash up.  Bang, crash, wallop went the china and pans, the utensils were dumped

carelessly, first in luke warm water and then in the draining rack, they felt sticky and dull

and the noise was deafening.     The trouble was that Madge, was having a non-creative

spell with her sculpting and this always made her feel at odds with the world.  The

Kitchen Crew knew it wouldn’t last long but it was awful while it did.  Henry very wisely

kept out of the way but this meant there was no calming influence in the kitchen and the

Kitchen Crew suffered in silence.  Even Crumpet the cat kept out of the way so he

couldn’t play his usual annoying game of batting members of the Crew under the cooker.

Eventually Madge finished the washing up to her satisfaction – even if it wasn’t to

anyone else’s and banging the door loudly went out to her barn to wait for inspiration to

come back.

                 There was a wonderful, peaceful silence in the kitchen.

                “Oh bliss!” said Holly, “I thought I’d split with all that noise!”

                  Laddie Ladle was tearful, “She banged me so hard I thought I was dented and

usually she’s so gentle!”

“It doesn’t happen often, luckily!” said Butts.

                  Wilma the Whisk was stunned, “Gee, that reminded me of a hurricane my

hardware store  was in once!  The sign blew away and the glass door smashed!”

                   Gradually the Kitchen Crew got back to normal, but those who had been

washed were not very happy.

             “I’m still sticky!” said Jenny Jamspoon “She didn’t use hot water and she didn’t

give me a single rub!”

             “I’ve got grease all over my uniform,” – that was General Carver grumpily “I’m

sure my blade has been dulled with all that thumping around!”

              Claude was incandescent with rage, “My points, they are in danger of being

blunted – the way they are treated, il est tres mal – I shall complain to …… someone!”

              Eventually everyone simmered down and they became aware of a monotonous

noise, a sort of drip, drip, drip.

             “What is that noise?” said Fillip, “It’s stopping me sleeping!”  Butts listened

carefully then swivelled round in his jam jar.

                “Oh no!” he exclaimed, “Madge has left the tap dripping – isn’t that what they

call Chinese water torture?”  “The plug is in too, lets hope Madge comes back before

long.”

                Suddenly there was a sliding, slithering noise followed by a little clunk as one

of the saucepans Madge had placed rather carelessly slipped and came to rest against the

tap.

                 This slight nudge was enough to move the tap so that the drip became a trickle.

The Kitchen Crew silence became uneasy as they wondered what they could do with their

puny strength.  None of them was strong enough to put the tap off or to pull out the plug,

what else could they do?  In the past making a noise had alerted Madge to danger but she

wasn’t there to hear any noise – they could all tap dance on the cooker and she wouldn’t

hear.

           “Madge is going to come back to a flooded kitchen if she doesn’t hurry up,” said

Laddie “What can we do?”

            “Can we get the water to run into the little sink next to the big one?” wondered Butts.

            They took it in turns to push the trickling tap but to no avail, it was quite stiff and

they were so weak.

             Then to the surprise of everyone little Tommy Teaspoon – still glorying in his

part in the rescue of the Bad Egg situation climbed out of his drawer and started hopping

up and down in excitement.  He was so excited he was babbling and developed a stutter

he had not had before!

              “I…..I….. know…..I know what to do.. do!” he twittered.  The bigger tools were

not used to small object thinking for themselves and were inclined to ignore little

Tommy.

            “Yeah, yeah honey!” said Wilma, “Sure you do – you go call Madge back, there’s

a good little feller!”

              There was muted laughter in the kitchen but everyone was too worried to be very

amused.

             “No, no!” stuttered Tommy “I really… really…. really… really do know what to do!”

             “Go on then,” said Fillip, “tell us your great idea.”  If the truth were told Fillip

was a bit peeved that he might be upstaged by little Tommy – still – if it worked it would

be worth it.

            “It’s us spoons!” said Tommy” We can do it!”

            “Do what?” asked Butts, mystified.

            “Us spoons –“ said Tommy ungrammatically, “Can empty the sink – well not

empty but try and get a lot of water out of the big sink and into the little one!”

             There was a stunned, admiring silence,

             “That is just brilliant!” said Fillip, forgetting his unwillingness to share the good

idea industry.

             “Quick, all the spoons, see what you can do!”

              The spoons lined up and one by one dipped their bowls into the fast filling sink

and offloaded into the little one next to it.  It was a big job and they were only slowing

down the inevitable when Fillip said,

                 “Come on I said ALL the spoons!”  No one else moved, Fillip was impatient,

“Laddie, you’re just a big spoon!” he shouted, “Get a move on, it may not be stew but its

in a good cause!”

                   Laddie roused himself from a reverie where he was surrounded by various

flavoured stews – Wilma having told him about Clam Chowder and Chilli Con Carne.

                 “Of course, sorry!” he jumped into the sink and started splashing the water

over the top to safety.

                “Hey watch out, you’re going to add to the flood like that not stop it!” said

Fillip, as water splashed around the floor and work surface.  “Sorry!” said Laddie “I’ll be

more careful!”

                   There was some confusion when Sophie Sieve leaped in and tried to help.

With all her holes she was worse than useless and had to be forcibly ejected by Laddie so

he could get on with the job.  When he tired the tablespoons and Server sisters Shirley

and Sharon took over to give him a break. But it was Laddie with his large bowl who

really saved the day and with breaks from the spoons now and again kept the water from

pouring over the edge.

                   They were all just about tired out when they heard a step out side the door

and Henry came in.  The Kitchen Crew lay down, still and watchful.  With an

exclamation Henry rushed to the tap and turned it off.  Just as everyone relaxed, the

unthinkable happened.

                   Laddie’s enthusiasm had slopped some water on the floor and as Henry

turned he slipped, cracked his head on the edge of the sink and knocked himself out!

Now the Kitchen Crew were faced with another problem, how to bring Henry round!

This time the answer was accidental.  As Laddie leaned over the edge of the sink to look

at Henry some drips of water fell from his bowl and Henry shivered as they splashed his

face.  Quickly Laddie scooped up some more water and tipped it down onto him.

Henry spluttered, opened his eyes and sat up.  He was too dazed to wonder why his face

was wet and later on that evening when the thought crossed his mind  he decided that the

only logical answer was concussion!

                      Madge was apologetic and vowed to be more careful in future, and as her

bad temper had been taken out on her latest sculpture in the barn she was extremely

caring of Henry’s health for the rest of the evening.

title: A New Addition
body:
Crumpet had been king of the kitchen until Fillip (with an F) Fork arrived and enlivened

the utensils.  He had never been very keen on this development, especially when he had

got the blame for some of their antics.  Now his self- esteem was to be dented even

further by the arrival of a new addition.

            Henry had wanted to give Madge a really special present for a long time, ever

since the disastrous silver cutlery fiasco in fact.  The perfect present idea occurred to him

one day when he walked into the kitchen so quietly that Madge did not hear him.  She

carried on doing what had become second nature to her – talking to the Kitchen Crew or

as it appeared to Henry to herself!  He watched and listened, amused for a moment.

             “I don’t know why you had to fall onto the floor, you silly spoon, when I’ve just

washed you!”  Sally Spoon was indignant.

              Silently she said, “I didn’t fall, you dropped me Madge because you never look

where you are putting things!   And you hadn’t washed me, just dipped me in water,

there’s still soup on my shoulder!”

                Yes it had been a soup day and Laddie was euphoric.  “I really did well, didn’t

I Madge? We didn’t have a single drip of soup anywhere!”

As if she heard him, Madge said, “That was a very successful meal Mr. Ladle (she didn’t

know he was called Laddie) we both enjoyed the soup and you were very deft as usual!”

             Henry cleared his throat, Madge jumped and looked round in embarrassment,

           “Oh Henry, you’ve caught me talking to the cutlery!” she exclaimed, “You must

think I’m going round the bend!”

            “Oh no my dear, not at all, in fact you have just given me the answer to a question

which has been worrying me lately!”

          Madge was mystified but no amount of begging would persuade Henry to tell her

what he meant.  He just tapped the side of his nose and smiled mysteriously.

           The next day Madge was in the kitchen washing up again, (where does it come

from?” she wondered) when Henry put his head around the door and instructed her to sit

down and close her eyes!  Madge loved a mystery so she did as she was bidden and

waited expectantly, privately she prepared herself to be grateful and enthusiastic

whatever the present was, she didn’t want to disappoint Henry as he clearly thought she

was going to like what he had bought her.

            Henry walked into the kitchen and put a piece of leather strap into Madge’s hand.

“A handbag?” she wondered, “A belt for my new dress?”

           “Open your eyes!” said Henry.

            Madge did so, in her hand was a strip of leather which went down almost to the floor.

            Madge gasped, “Oh Henry, Henry!” she cried in delight, “It’s perfect! – eh what

is it?” It was recognisably a puppy but the breed was not clear.  Long black ears nearly

touched the ground, the body was long and so was the nose but the legs were ridiculously

short and the eyes – the eyes were the saddest, most soulful she had ever seen. Suddenly

Madge realised – “It’s a Daxi!” she shouted “Oh how wonderful, just like the one I had as

a child – and yes I did talk to him, he was so intelligent.  What is his name?”

                “I left that to you,” said Henry “He had some long, Kennel Club name but you

wouldn’t want to use that every day.  What do you think would suit him?”

              Madge looked at the pup long and hard.  He looked back solemnly waiting to be

christened.

            “I know,” said Madge at last, “He reminds me of an uncle of mine.  We’ll

call him Wilfrid!”  The puppy wagged his tail – he approved!  It was a strong, silent,

important kind of name.

               The Kitchen Crew had mixed feelings. Madge would now talk to the dog

instead of them!  And what if he turned out to be another spiteful Crumpet? But they

need not have worried.  Wilfred would prove to be the opposite of the cat. He was kind,

gentle and as helpful as a rather thick Dachshund could be.  For Wilfrid was not exactly

like Madge’s childhood pet.  Wilfrid was definitely one sandwich short of a picnic but

this made him all the more endearing!  You just wanted to look after him and the Kitchen

Crew was soon as smitten with the new arrival as Madge was.

          Needless to say, Crumpet was NOT IMPRESSED.  The first time he came through

the cat flap and face to face with Wilfrid he was nonplussed. He could not believe his

amber eyes. A dog!  In his kitchen!  Dogs chase cats right?  Crumpet prepared to sacrifice

his dignity and flee.  But there was no need with a delighted snuffle Wilfrid stretched out

his long nose to the cat, the cat raised a paw, claws extended ready to do battle.  Wilfrid

sneezed.  Crumpet was appalled, how frightfully rude!  Before he could recover Wilfrid

had chucked the cat under the chin with his nose and delivered a long wet lick that

encompassed Crumpet’s face.  The cat was baffled, dogs were supposed to either chase

cats or run away when the cat chased them.  They were definitely not supposed to greet

them like long loss friends whose welcome is assured.

                    It took Crumpet a long time to get used to Wilfrid, he half expected him to

revert to type at anytime but Wilfrid was consistent, he loved Crumpet unconditionally

and wanted only to be friends.  The Kitchen Crew was amused.  It was funny to see their

old enemy, Crumpet, so outfaced and not knowing what to do but at least if his time was

taken up with the dog he would have less time to bat Fillip and his friends under the

furniture.

                  Wilfrid accepted the liveliness of the Kitchen Crew without reserve.  They

would tease  him by suddenly moving when his wet nose investigated one of them and

Wilfrid would play his part by pretending to be surprised and leaping backwards.

The puppy was only two months old and had a lot of learning to do. Sometimes learning

was painful.  Madge smacked him (rather half heartedly) when he chewed something he

shouldn’t but then excused him by saying he was only a baby and probably teething and

cuddled him to make up for the smack.  Henry didn’t smack, he waggled his finger and

boomed at Wilfrid who had the sense to look chastened and apologetic and thus got away

with murder!

           Wilfrid had a huge appetite.  It took Madge and Henry a while to realise that

Crumpet wasn’t complaining for nothing, he was slowly fading away (he would have

said) because Wilfrid emptied not only his own ample dish but Crumpet’s as well.

Crumpet had been used to leaving a morsel in his dish “for later.”  He could no longer

afford to do this as his bit for later became Wilfrid’s bit for now – in case he was never

fed again.

Eventually Madge realised what was happening and gave Crumpet a small area of work

                                                                            78

surface near the boiler for a dining room; it was too hot there for any food preparation so

hygiene was kept up.  It also had the advantage as far as Crumpet was concerned of not

only preserving his food (though now it got dried up if he left it for too long) but giving

him the height from which he could look down on Wilfrid and at least pretend that he

was superior.  Wilfrid didn’t mind looking up at Crumpet he adored him and thought he

was wonderful anyway.  Let’s face it, Wilfrid’s mother had done a very poor job when it

came to educating her son about the natural order of things in the animal world.

Wilfrid seemed to like the frolics of the Kitchen Crew.  He had a padded basket in the

kitchen and would watch with interest as they played.  He didn’t join in exactly but

would probe them with his nose if they stayed still for too long.  Butts Butterknife would

have a cause to be glad of Wilfrid’s concern.

            The puppy’s interest in food extended beyond his and Crumpet’s food bowls.

Anything slightly edible was worth investigating – including, unfortunately, rubbish

bags.  Madge learned quickly on this one!  She used supermarket bags behind a unit door

for her rubbish, when full they were tied up and then sat around for a while until she

remembered to put them in the rubbish bin.  A couple of bags, “investigated” by Wilfrid,

contents strewn around the kitchen, soon encouraged her to be more careful and for a

while no bags were left in Wilfrid’s reach.

            But Madge was prone to slip up on good intentions and after several days when

nothing was left to chance once again a bag was left on the floor.  The rubbish bag, full,

but not yet with its handles tied sat on the floor next to the sink.  As Madge turned she

accidentally knocked Butts off the work surface and straight into the half open rubbish
.
bag.  He fell with hardly a sound into the middle of a pile of potato peelings.

            “Ugh!” said Butts, “that feels horrible, wet and squishy!”  He lay for a while

waiting for Madge to realise what had happened – but she didn’t.  In fact she added more

crumbs, a half eaten piece of toast and the dried up left overs of Crumpet’s dish to the

bag before tying the handle and placing the bag next to the back door.

            Fillip who had seen what happened was alarmed.  When the kitchen was empty he

ran over to the rubbish bag and called to Butts, “Can you hear me, Butts?  Where are

you?”

            The voice was muffled, “I’m in the middle of a pile of potato peelings!  I’m

wedged and I can’t get out!  Help me Fillip or I’ll be thrown out and taken away by the

dustmen tomorrow!”

            Fillip scrabbled uselessly at the plastic bag, he was not strong enough to make a

hole because every time he pushed his prongs into it the soft contents of the bag gave and

no holes resulted.

            Twice Madge came into the kitchen, twice she forgot to put the rubbish bag out

and twice she picked up Fillip and put him back in the sink, it was all very frustrating.

            Then Madge ushered Wilfrid into the kitchen, told him to be a good boy, closed

the door and forgot once more about the rubbish.

            Fillip seized his chances, “Wilfrid,” he said, and received a thorough licking from

the tail wagging pup. “Yuk, stop it, you daft dog, I haven’t any meat on me!  How would

you like to help our friend Butts?”  Wilfrid woofed his agreement. “See that rubbish bag

over there!  By the back door?  Can you break into it and set Butts free, Madge knocked

him in there by mistake and didn’t notice!”

           Wilfrid’s eager expression changed to one of alarm.

          “Can’t!” he woofed.  “Wilfie get smacked if Wilfie spread rubbish round one more

time!”

            Fillip was baffled, he certainly didn’t blame Wilf for wanting to avoid that

punishment.  Not that Madge’s smacks hurt, they were more like hard pats really, it was

the thought that Madge would be hurt at his naughtiness and have to do extra housework

(which she hated) cleaning up the mess.

          “Tell you what,” said Fillip “Just break the bag open so Butts can get out, you

don’t have to spread the rubbish around. “In fact,” he added with an inspiration, “We’ll

get Butts to stay there just in sight so Madge will see him.  She likes Butts and will be

glad to rescue him!”

            So Wilfrid very carefully attacked the plastic bag until there was a tear just big

enough for Butts to put his head out, no peelings tipped out.  Butts lay there and everyone

waited expectantly.  Eventually Madge returned, spotted the bag, torn but not

8dismembered and her favourite knife sticking out.  She was so apologetic to Butts and

only half told Wilfrid off for tearing the bag.

           Butts and Fillip thanked Wilfrid profusely when she had gone, taking the rubbish

with her. “Sorlright!” said Wilfrid shyly, “Cwumpet would have done the same!”

          “Eh, well, no actually” said Butts, “There’s a lot you have to learn about Cwumpet,

eh Crumpet – he’d have taken the bag with Butts in it out to the bin himself  if he could!”

But Wilfrid was not convinced, “Cwumpet nice,” he said “Cwumpet Wilfie’s fwiend!”

And there the Kitchen Crew had to leave it, Wilfrid would learn soon enough

title: On Holiday – Poor Fillip
body:
Henry had gone quite mad!  He loved to save money and he loved going on holiday.  The

two didn’t usually go together – until Henry bought a caravan from a friend.  Madge

wasn’t sure.  She liked holidays too – but in hotels where someone else was responsible

for the cooking, washing up and cleaning, she didn’t think it would be much of a holiday

for HER in a caravan – it would be like living and sleeping in a kitchen half the size of

her own!  “We’ll just try it and see how it goes,” said Henry encouragingly “I’ll help with

the chores and we’ll eat out too!  The good thing is we can take Wilfrid with us!”  So

Madge was convinced.

            They loaded up the caravan with the minimum of cutlery and Fillip was delighted

to be included.  The caravan was duly hitched up and they set off.  The first holiday was

to be spent in Norfolk where they thought they might spend a day on a boat.  They could

also introduce Wilfrid to the delights of swimming – however, this proved a non-starter.

Wilfrid did not like water and in his desperation to avoid being drowned soaked Madge

and Henry so that they had to give up and dry off.

             Madge had packed a picnic so they could eat lunch on the way without losing too

much time and also to get in the mood for eating al fresco.  Wilfrid was made very

comfortable in the back of the estate car with a cushion, water and doggy chew but

fenced of from the rest of the car by a dog guard.  Henry was taking no chances with his

upholstery in case Wilf felt a teething attack coming on.

             At lunch-time Henry parked in a large lay-by and they walked a few yards into a

wood before finding a pleasant glade.  Madge laid out the lunch on the picnic table while
.
Henry fussed with folding chairs – he wasn’t going completely native and had brought

quite a few home comforts – much to Madge’s secret amusement.  Wilfrid, after a good

walk and a sniffing of many trees, was tied up to a clever metal gadget that screwed into

the ground which gave him a big circle to wander in.  After getting tangled round

Henry’s chair and nearly causing him to lose his dignity Wilfrid was placed further away

and felt a bit left out and sad.  Fillip tried to cheer him up by waving and making faces at

him whenever Henry and Madge weren’t looking.  Wilfrid had finished up all the scraps

likely to appeal to a dog and quite a few that dogs weren’t expected to eat.  Henry called

him “Waste Bin Wilfrid” but quite affectionately and Madge hugged him and told him he

was a clever boy – quite why when all he had done was try to fill his ample and elastic

stomach Wilf didn’t know, but he liked the hugs.

            Everything was loaded into picnic bags and then into the car and they prepared to

set off on the final leg of the journey.  Henry had no sooner engaged gear and started to

pull away when he became aware of a commotion in the back.  Wilfrid was looking out

of the window, pacing up and down and barking – very unlike his usual quiet, dignified

self.

            Henry stopped the car, “What’s the matter with the pup?” he asked.

            Madge looked around, puzzled.  “I don’t know,” she said.  “He’s not caught up on

anything, he has his cushion – I don’t know what’s wrong but something is bothering

him!”

           They got out of the car and opened up the tailgate.  Wilfrid was relieved and very

proud to have had a suitable effect because he had a very good reason for his action.  He

alone had spotted that Fillip had been left behind!  Looking through the back window as

they set off he had seen his friend running hopelessly on his little short legs and waving

his arms about.  Henry would have been mortified when he found out his loss but

probably wouldn’t have gone back all the way just for a fork.

            Now on his release from the car Wilfrid bounded back to the picnic spot and

picked up     Fillip in his big soft mouth.

          “Oh Wilfrid, thank you so much,” gasped Fillip, “I was really afraid I would never

see you or the Kitchen Crew again!”

“Fiwip, Wilfie’s fwend!” said Wilfrid indistinctly

through his mouthful.

Madge and Henry were astounded when they saw what Wilfrid had picked up.  They

immediately revised their opinion of his intelligence and treated him almost as an equal,

even including him in their conversation for the rest of the journey.

Wilfrid was bewildered, he had only rescued his friend after all and though he

appreciated the extra attention he didn’t understand a word of what they said.  But his

occasional woof seemed to be in the right places so that Madge and Henry began to think

they had a genius on their hands who might even be entered in a Most Intelligent Dog

Show somewhere!

At the campsite the caravan was quickly positioned and an awning brought out ready to

be added on. Then Henry discovered there was no mallet with the awning and the ground

was too hard just to push in the guy rope pegs.  He was frustrated and just as he was

beginning to give up, hoping it would not be too windy for the awning Madge saved the
                                                                    84
day!

            Rather shamefaced she handed Henry the perfect thing for solving his problem.

Henry was bemused, as he looked down at Roland the Rolling Pin. “ What on earth did

you bring that for Madge?  We’re supposed to be travelling light!”

            “Well you never know” said Madge vaguely, then brightly – “You never knew

you would need something like that did you?  So I was right after all!”  Henry had no

answer to that  and reflected that Madge’s eccentricities could come in useful!

            Roland’s feelings were mixed.  He too was baffled by his inclusion on the holiday.

            “Perhaps she wants me as a possible weapon, if we’re attacked,” he mused “I am

the biggest and heaviest thing around after all.”  He and Ted were always arguing about

this.

            He wasn’t too sure about being taken for a mallet.  For one thing tent pegs were a

lot harder than the squishy pastry he was usually used on and it gave him quite a

headache.

            On the other hand he would be able to lord it over those left behind.  Of course,

Fillip would be able to tell them the true story. He wondered if he could bribe Fillip to

put a better light on things?  No, he decided Fillip was incorruptible but might be

persuaded to say nothing, just look enigmatic maybe – like Henry did sometimes when

faced with one of Madge’s crazy sculptures.

             Someone else who wasn’t too happy with the camping arrangements was Kneil Knife.

            Henry was one tent peg short for his awning and had had to use Kneil instead,

reckoning they would not use him if they were going to eat out,  Kneil, who had been so

happy to be included was now not so sure!  Would he be thought dual-purpose he

wondered?  If so, he could stand up to Claude the Cheese knife when he got back, it

would be worth being head down in the cold ground for a few hours just to see Claude’s

expression when he was told he was no longer unique amongst knives.  With that happy

thought Kneil decided to make the best of his lowly position.

            The next morning after breakfast Henry and Madge went shopping at the caravan

site shop and bought some spare tent pegs.  So Kneil was relieved of his humble job and

returned to the cutlery drawer, but decided to make the most of his experience when he

got home.  He could see his fascinated audience, listening in delight to his story of the

holiday.  “There I was,” he would say, “The only useful thing in the caravan to help

Henry out of his trouble!  Well of course, I couldn’t refuse, could I?  “That’s OK Henry,”

I said, “Anytime I can be of help, old boy just let me know!”  And so I spent the WHOLE

night helping to keep the awning safe and stop it blowing away.” (It had actually been a
2
very still night, but a wind MIGHT have blown up!)

            Fillip mused that when they did get home there would be several stories to tell

already and they were only on the first day!  He shivered as he remembered his narrow

escape from being left behind and wondered if all the members of the Kitchen Crew who

were camping would make it home.  He hoped Henry’s carefulness would make up for

Madge’s scattiness. Of course, now they had Wilfrid to help them!

title: Meanwhile – Back at the Farm
body:
The Kitchen Crew settled down for a quiet few days, nothing much would happen while

Henry and Madge were away – would it?  If anything it would be down to Butts who had

somehow become Fillip’s right hand man to sort it out.  He hoped it would be a quiet

time, he didn’t feel as clever as Fillip seemed to be and he was nervous.

            Crumpet was being fed by a neighbour and had a comfortable chair complete with

cushion in the greenhouse.  Henry had bolted the cat flap in case there were any very

small burglars about, or maybe to stop Crumpet bringing his friends into the kitchen for a

rave up.  He needn’t have worried, Crumpet had no friends only deadly enemies amongst

other cats and thorns in the flesh otherwise known as the Kitchen Crew.  Well that wasn’t

quite true Crumpet’s one true friend was Madge.  She saw only the furry bundle who

rubbed around her legs when he was hungry and purred on her lap when it was cold

outside.  So Madge had a last minute pang of conscience.  What if it was cold and wet

when they were away?  Poor little Crumpet would be miserable so kind hearted Madge

undid Henry’s good work and released the bolt on the cat flap. Now Crumpet and any

small burglars could enter the house at their leisure.

            While she waited for Henry to load and hitch up the caravan Madge had a last

minute cup of coffee in the garden.  Idly she wandered around dead-heading here and

pruning there, she was unaware that as she put her cup and saucer on the ground for the

fifth time, little Tommy Teaspoon slipped over the edge of the saucer and lay unnoticed

in the grass.

            Henry called her and Madge hurried into the kitchen carefully locking and bolting

the back door while ignoring the now unlocked cat flap.  Henry, Madge and Wilfrid went

on holiday and the rest of the Kitchen Crew settled down for a quiet time.

             It took Tommy several hours to get as far as the back door and by the time he

arrived there he had had enough adventures to keep the rest of the Kitchen Crew awake

for several nights.  Tommy was a very small teaspoon and his little legs were tiny, so

meetings with slugs and snails were terrifying!  Not that they were interested in Tommy,

since he wasn’t edible they let him pass unscathed.  The trouble with Tommy was that in

spite of being very small physically, his imagination was huge!  This caused him many

moments of sheer terror as he made his way back to the kitchen.  Not that getting back to

the house helped much as he could not get in!

            Crumpet passed him on his way to check out his cat flap and barely gave him a

glance, “Long way from home aren’t you, Shorty?” was all he said.  “Could you get me

into the kitchen Crumpet, its lonely out here!”  Crumpet was amused.  “I don’t think so,

Shorty, my pockets are rather full!”  And with a sneer Crumpet jumped elegantly and

noisily through the flap.  He was disappointed to find no food waiting for him and then

remembered the neighbour who was going to feed him outside. As he turned to go back

through the flap he noticed Teddy Teaspoon looking sad.  “Lost your brother have you?”

He asked with a sneer, “How did you know?” asked Teddy in surprise.  Crumpet was

curt, “Well there’s someone outside who looks like you trying to get through my cat flap.

Fat chance!”

             The flap clanged to behind the cat and Teddy started running round in small

circles and wringing his hands.

“Oh no, oh no!” he wailed, “Poor Tommy! He’ll be so lonely and frightened outside on

his own, he’s an awful coward!”

Butts heart sank.  This was what he had been dreading – a crisis and no Fillip around to

sort it out.  The spoons came to him, - a delegation!  “Well Butts,” they said “You’re in

charge what are you going to do about it?  One of our family needs help and there is only

you with enough brains to do anything!”

            Butts nearly panicked but took himself in hand and tried to think logically.  The

back door was hopeless, it was locked.  The cat flap was unlocked but too heavy for the

Kitchen Crew to lift.  Crumpet was the only one who could open it and he wasn’t exactly

helpful. He had been tricked into helping before and was now very wary.  But when he

had been fed outside he would be back to spend the night in comfort on the sofa.  Butts

reckoned he had an hour or so to work out a plan and get some of the Kitchen Crew to

help.  The Kitchen Crew was an easy matter, most of them were always ready to help and

the others could usually be persuaded or urged!

            First Butts went to the back-door which had a small gap under it and called out to

Tommy.  Poor little Tommy who thought he was doomed to spend a lonely few days

outside was pathetically grateful to hear Butts’ voice and pleased to find HE was going to

be the centre of a rescue plan!  He wouldn’t have thought he was important enough!

            Butts reassured him, “You’re one of the Kitchen Crew just as much as any of us

                                                                        88

Tommy,” he said, “And we all look out for each other!”

            “Now,” he continued, “All you have to do is wait until the cat flap is open and be

ready to do as you’re told!”

            Tommy was eager and excited and very, very proud!

            Next Butts had to find just the right utensils to do the first part of the job.  He

wanted two tall strong, well behaved types.  Well, of course, no contest there.  He

approached General Carver and Colonel Bread. They were so bored with no enemies to

fight they were delighted to help.  Though General Carver did have to make a big

production out of it.

            He hum’d and haa’d a bit before agreeing though he was careful not to draw it out

too long in case Butts thought of asking someone else.

            The next job was quite daunting, he had to get Anna and Belle to agree not only to

help but with each other!  So he had to be crafty!  Speaking to both, since they were

joined he didn’t have much choice there.  He said casually, “I don’t support you two

would be interested in helping rescue young Tommy Teaspoon?”

            “Why not?” said Anna crossly.

            “Well it would mean co-operating with Belle for a start!”

            “She might have a problem with that, I don’t!”

            Belle was hurt, “I’ll do anything for that little fellow,” she said, “even if it does

mean working with HER!”

            Now Butts had the fairly easy job of rounding up everybody, anyone would do to

support the main players.  There was no shortage of offers of help; it seemed everyone

had a soft spot for Tommy.

            General Carver and Colonel Bread took up their positions on either side of the cat

flap, careful not to be close enough to be knocked over when Crumpet made his usual

dramatic – “Look at me, how clever I am!” – entrance.  To one side were Anna – Belle

and various members of the knife, fork and spoon family.  The bottom of the cat flap was

about 10cm off the floor and was quite high for most of the Crew, they stood around,

looking up at it in awe.

            They had not long to wait.  Crumpet, more or less satisfied with the contents of

his meal (though  the surrounds of the garden was not up to that of the warm kitchen)

pushed his way through his own private doorway and stepped daintily into the kitchen.

He stopped warily, surrounded by what looked like the entire Kitchen Crew standing in a

circle round him and just looking expectantly at him,

            “What?” he said crossly, “Now what?”

            “Nothing!” said Butts and just waited.  Crumpet continued to squeeze his long

body into the kitchen looking all the while for any booby traps   which might have been

set for him.

            Nothing happened so he carried on heading for the comfort of the living room

sofa.  He stopped, something was wrong, different – what?  Of course, he had not heard

the clang of the cat flap closing behind him.  He whirled round expecting trouble and saw

an strange sight.
2
            His cat flap was still open, held in position from the sides, their points inclined to

the middle by General Carver and Colonel Bread.  Anna-Belle was climbing up and

stopped with Anna one side and Belle the other.  Now a couple of spoons scrambled up

Belle and down Anna on the other side.  Tommy was waiting, they grabbed him and

pushing and hauling got him up Anna and down Belle and safely into the kitchen.

Everyone heaved a sigh of relief.  Someone helped Anna and Belle down and General

Carver and Colonel Bread  let the cat flap go with a clang.  Everyone cheered.  Even

Crumpet looked impressed and muttered.

            “Well done, you work together very well!”

              Butts was applauded for his idea and seemed about to burst with pride.  He could

look Fillip in the eyes on his return all right and report, “All present and correct Sir!”

title: Smell Aversion!
body:
If Butts thought his problems were over he was mistaken!  After sleeping very soundly

having had great rescue type dreams he awoke to the silent kitchen.  Silent?  Too silent!

It was very quiet and after enjoying it for a while Butts started to worry. He could not for

the life of him figure out what was wrong but something was, what on earth was it?

He thought about all the things in the kitchen which might be expected to break the

silence.  There was the fan oven which hummed, but of course nothing was cooking.

There was the washing machine, not making splashing and whirring noises.  The

microwave wasn’t buzzing.  The kettle wasn’t bubbling.  Crumpet had gone out so he

wasn’t purring.  The fridge-freezer wasn’t rumbling because Henry had gone round

switching everything off.  Of course!  Henry had been altogether too clever this time!  He

had switched the fridge-freezer off without checking the contents and while the fridge

had been run low to hold a minimum of foodstuffs the freezer was usually well stocked.

Not only would the contents thaw and be spoiled there would be the most awful smell

because Madge had harvested broccoli from her garden before going away and stored it,

neatly bagged and labelled in the freezer.  Once before a small piece of broccoli had

fallen behind the cooker and caused an awful ‘pong’ before it was found.  The quantity

Madge had frozen (an unusually housewifely thing for her to do!) would fill the whole

house with an aroma no one could live with!

            Butts was stumped this time.  If Madge came home and found her one trip into

this kind of  housekeeping had been a disaster she might never try again!  Butts sought

help from wise Wilma the Whisk; she had been invaluable in the past with her somewhat

wider knowledge of the world.

            Wilma was flattered to be asked to help Butts and made a bit of a meal of it.  “

Well now let me see honey,” she began.  “When I was in the hardware store, there were

lots of appliances there, some plugged in and switched on and some not but I never had

occasion to do anything with them.  I’ve got a feeling switches are rather hard to deal

with if the big click they make is anything to go by!”

            Butts sighed, “I think you’re right, but I’ll have to try anyway. The fridge-freezer

was tall but the socket into which it was plugged was fairly easy to reach as it was at the

back of the unit next to it.  Butts hauled himself up the unit, using handles and knobs –

the usual way the Kitchen Crew got themselves anywhere.  The switches – two of them –

were both up.  Half heartedly Butts tried to press one down.  It was impossible, much too

stiff for his puny strength to make any difference. Once again it would have to be a

combined effort of the Kitchen Crew, but he couldn’t think how, there wasn’t room for

more than one to press down on the switch and he didn’t see how else it could be done.

Wilma was no help, who else could be ask?

            The Sharp Army was no use.  “If you wanted to put it off we could cut the cable,”

said Sergeant All Purpose, “But that doesn’t help does it?”

“No!” sighed Butts.  “But thank you anyway.”

            The knives, forks and spoons were glad to offer their help and encouragement but

had no new ideas.  The wooden spoons he asked just so they wouldn’t feel left out, they

were useless.  So were Sophie Sieve, Peter Pallet and Laddie Ladle. Claude he asked as a

last resort.  He was more willing than usual having been humbled by the obvious dislike

of the rest of the crew, but even he didn’t have much to offer, “If you need me, mon ami,

I will gladly cut and slice and pick up with my sharp points, but there is nothing else I can

do.  Sometimes even being dual purpose is not enough!”

            Butts was stumped he sought out Topsy Corkscrew, not that she could do

anything but he

needed someone to tell him he had done his best and not to worry.  Topsy did that, “’Ere

chuck doncha worry your pretty little ‘ed no more,” (She’d opened a bottle of beer for

Henry as he didn’t drink wine in the middle of the day.)  “You done your best I’m sure!”

They were sitting on the unit comforting each other when there was a loud cough from

above them.  They looked up to see Ted Tenderiser looking down at them from the top of

the fridge-freezer where Madge had left him in one of her (many) absent minded

moments.

“Oh hello, Ted!” Butts was embarrassed, he had forgotten Ted.  “Out of sight, out of

mind I suppose!”  he said, hurt.  “I heard you asking everyone else for help – everyone

else except me!” he emphasised.

“Sorry Ted, I didn’t think there was anything you could do – I don’t think brute strength

is the answer to this problem!”

“There I beg to differ, my lad!” said Ted haughtily, “Some of us have ideas as well as

strength!  As I see it you need a bit of weight to knock that switch down, and I am the

heaviest member in the kitchen!”

Butts brightened, Ted had a point.  “Go on then Ted,” he said eagerly, “You’re right, lets

see what you can do!”  Ted peered over the top of the fridge-freezer and tried to judge the

angle, if he missed he’d have to climb up and try again. Even though he was heavy he

had no more strength than the rest of the crew!  He disappeared to the back of the fridge-

freezer and they could hear him counting,

            “One, two, three, here we go!”  Ted sailed through the air and gave the switch a

glancing blow before landing heavily, nearly on top of Topsy who squealed in alarm,”

            “Cor love a duck Ted, you nearly ‘ad me then!”

            “Sorry,” said Ted, panting a bit, “Did it work?” they all rushed across to the

switch, it was maddeningly half way down but still not enough to put the power through,

and there was no hum from the fridge-freezer.

            “Well,” said Butts, “it’s better than it was, we’re halfway there!”

             Ted was gloomy,  “ I don’t think it would help to try jumping off again,” he said

“As it is I think I dented the counter top!”  You don’t want too many marks like that

when they come back. They might wonder what on earth had happened here.  I could

even get my marching orders!”

            “Never mind, lets think how we can finish the job.”

             Topsy suddenly got quite excited, “’ere!” she said “I fink I’ve gorn and done it! I

fink I’ve ‘ad an idea!”  “Go on then! Share it with us!” said a somewhat disbelieving

Butts.

            Topsy pointed to the switch, “I reckon if Ted just leaned on the switch, even if

‘e’s not strong enough to move it on ‘is own, we could kind of jump up and down on ‘im

and give ‘im an extra bit of weight like!”

            Ted was not very pleased at the idea of anyone jumping up and down on him but

he had to agree he could reach the switch with his knobbly head and Butts and Topsy

jumping together might have the desired effect. Rather reluctantly he went across to the

switch and placed himself, bracing his little arms and legs so he wouldn’t slip off the

switch and dent the counter again.

            Carefully, hand in hand Butts and Topsy edged their way up Ted’s handle. The

rest of the Kitchen Crew scrambled round the kitchen trying to find the best vantage

points and started chanting.

            “Bounce Butts, jump Topsy, bounce Butts, jump Topsy …….”  they did.

Gradually Ted’s knobbly head brought the switch lower and lower – it was really very

stiff!  At last there was a click, the switch was on low as it would go.  They all let out a

cheer and started dancing around until Butts held up a hand.

            “Wait a minute,” he said, “listen!”  There was silence.  Nothing, no hum, no

rumble, there was no sound from the fridge-freezer!  Butts was baffled, then he sighed

            “You know what?” he said “that was the wrong switch! That’s the one the kettle

is plugged into.  The fridge-freezer is the one next to it – you can follow the cable and see

what I mean!”

            Everyone groaned. Ted was resigned, “Never mind,” he said, “I’m game to have

another go!”

            Once again Ted climbed on top of the fridge-freezer, aimed and jumped, once

more the switch went half way down!  There was a mixture of cheers and groans from the

Crew.

            Ted leaned his head on the switch and Butts and Topsy began their strange, crazy

looking dance.  Click  ----  whirr!  The fridge-freezer started up and there was  delight

echoing round the kitchen.  The Kitchen Crew had saved the day again.  Henry had
.
probably not even noticed that he had switched the freezer off and Madge would never

know what the Kitchen Crew had done to save her broccoli and a lot of cleaning.  Butts

sighed, “It would be nice, in a way if Madge knew about us,” he thought “but its not

really on, I suppose, we’ll just have to carry on, unrecognised, unrewarded and
