unappreciated!  Ah well!

                                                                     95

,n

title: A Dark and Gloomy Place
body:
Madge and Henry’s kitchen was part of an old farmhouse, bought because Madge wanted

the space afforded by the barns for her sculpture. Henry had agreed to the purchase a

little reluctantly, he really didn’t like spending large quantities of money, but he realised

if they bought a smaller house Madge’s sculptures would be brought inside and gradually

take over. For Madge did not do things by halves.  Her sculptures did not consist of neat

pieces of stone or marble.  Her style was modern - plus!  Most of her work consisted of

large pieces of scrap metal, often welded – noisily  together – Henry could live without

the noise and the smell of hot metal in his kitchen.

           So the kitchen was old fashioned but with modern units sitting rather uneasily

around the walls.  Because the farmhouse was old the walls and floor were uneven and

the modern units did not fit that well, this resulted in some dark, almost unreachable

corners.  The Kitchen Crew dreaded being dropped into these places.  They knew Madge

would not notice, probably would not miss them and if she did would have no idea where

to look.

            Major Fruit and Veg was the smallest of the Sharp Army.  He was quite popular

with Madge because of his size but he realised he was not unique. Madge sometimes used

Sergeant All Purpose for fruit and veg if she couldn’t put her hand on the Major.  So,

because of his size and because he would not be missed, the Major dreaded the dark and

gloomy places more than most.  Several times he had found himself on the edge of an

abyss and held his breath until he was moved to safety.  So it came as no surprise one day

when his worst fears were realised and a series of nudges – from Madge to Colonel

Bread, to Sophie Sieve and Wilma Whisk and finally to the Major meant he disappeared

down one of these dreaded spaces into no-mans land.

            Wilma was shocked to be the last in the chain which sent the Major to his doom

and she was determined to get him back – by hook or by crook, as she put it.  But, what

could they do?  Any other member of the Kitchen Crew going down after the Major

would merely keep him company.  Being the back of the units, there were no knobs or

handles to climb up, just sheer walls on both sides.

            Major Fruit and Veg were prepared to give up hope and spend the rest of his time

alone and useless. He pictured the rest of the gang, at first they would miss him and try to

think of ways to save him.  Then they would gradually forget him and he would be alone

for evermore.

   But he bargained without  his friend’s ingenuitylma was determined that this rescue

would be on her, as she put it.  Partly because she felt responsible and at least partly

because she didn’t want all the glory to go to “those Limeys”  - “Yanks have brains and

ingenuity too,” she said to herself and began to look around the kitchen for inspiration.

One by one she considered the other utensils and discarded them.  They were just too

small. The longest thinnest thing in the kitchen was the handle of the broom and it was

far too heavy for them to manoeuvre into place. Even if they had been able to poke it into

the dark space without injuring the Major, there were no hand holes on it for him to climb

up.

Kitchen Crew abandoned, other kitchen tools discounted Wilma was about to give up in

despair when the beginnings of an idea began to germinate in her mind.  On the fridge-

freezer doors were some magnets, some large some quite small.  One in particular caught

her eye, it was a little windmill with sails, they did not go round but they looked “kinda”

useful.  Wilma knew the Knife Army were a little different from her less sharp friends.

They were stainless steel and thus very magnetic.  Now all she had to do was to get the

fridge magnet to stick to the Major – and then – something!  Wilma’s imagination had

worn out so she called a meeting.  “Anyone who wants to rescue the Major, please meet

in the middle of the kitchen floor in half an hour, “ she announced.  Well, of course

everyone came, the Kitchen Crew loved a good rescue plan, and after all it might be one

of them in trouble one day!

Wilma explained the plan she had initiated using a fridge magnet and begged for help

with the minor details – like how they could actually use the magnet effectively!

There wasn’t much help forth coming!  Everyone seemed to be clean out of ideas and

Butts having handled two rescues was totally flummoxed.  His previous successful

attempts had made him look forward to Fillip’s return, now he wasn’t so sure, he didn’t

want to announce any failure.  He didn’t mind if someone else had an idea rather than

him, just as long as someone did – and soon!

“What is it you really want Wilma?” asked Jenny Jamspoon.  Wilma waved her arms

around rather vaguely – “Oh, you know, honey .…..” she said helplessly,  “Just

something to use with the magnet to get the Major out of trouble!”

“Suppose,” said Jenny, “we could tie something to the windmill magnet, let it down into

the dark space, let it grab the Major and pull him up – would that do?”

There was a collective gasp around the room!  Jenny was not known for her perspicacity!

She was a very sweet person naturally but not terribly well endowed with brain cells.

She was an only one, after all.  The other spoons had a collective brain (and that wasn’t

too brilliant) but poor Jenny was on her own.  Now, what she said made so much sense

that no one could quite believe it and everyone fell silent while they looked for flaws in

her argument.  There was only one, voiced by Peter Pallet – also a one off – he had a lot

of sympathy for Jenny.  He said what everyone was thinking, “What?” he said.

Jenny had not finished, she was ready for that one.

“In my drawer,” she said, “is some string if one of the Sharp Army could cut off a long

piece and if some of the stronger Crew could lower it down and pick up the Major

……ah ...well….you know….. it would work wouldn’t it?”

“It sure would honey,” said Wilma, “and you can go to the top of the class for ingenuity!”

Jenny blushed and enjoyed the admiring looks from her friends.

General Carver cleared his throat importantly.  Inwardly he was cross that one of his

army had not thought of the brilliant plea but at least they could save the day by

executing it.

“Right,” he said “well I think it is only right if the Major’s colleague takes over here!

Captain Boning – you will position yourself across the gap while Colonel Bread and I

take the weight, lower down the magnet and pull the Major up!”

“Sir, permission to speak sir!” Sergeant All Purpose wasn’t about to be left out of this

army exercise.

“Yes Sergeant?” General Carver was wary he didn’t want to be upstaged again.

“Permission to have the honour of cutting the string sir?”

“Very well Sergeant if you wish!” said the General magnanimously

The string was duly cut and tied around the little magnet which was surprisingly strong

and had taken a lot of shifting from its spot on the fridge door.  “Just in case, tie a loop at

the other end,” said Wilma, “that way you’ll have something to hang on to!”

Captain Boninger lay across the gap as best he could while the General and the Colonel

shouted instructions to the Major, lowered the magnet and started to haul.  There was a

hitch the first time when the Major fell off and landed on the floor again.  Then he

realised he had to hold on and not rely on the magnet because he was quite heavy and the

whole process started again.  Gradually he was hauled up and at last his head appeared

over the top of the unit.  There was a cheer all round as he clambered the last few inches.

Everyone congratulated everyone else.

“Do you realise,” said Wilma, “that with a few exceptions – she glanced meaningfully at

the Wooden Spoons, “we can rescue anyone that happens to, in the future – we can all

relax a little.  “Though,” she added, “we would prefer it, General Carver if we didn’t have

to haul you up – you’re so heavy – and we really need you in the rescue team!”  The

General swelled his chest in pride and decided not to put the Major on a charge for

desertion!”

Butts who had taken very little part in the rescue and was just a bit put out found himself

wishing very hard that the camping holiday was over and Fillip would come back to take

care of things, though he thought we have managed pretty well without him.  But still – I

miss him!

title: The Camping Holiday    Part 2
body:
Madge was preparing a picnic hamper. No pub lunch today, no caravan lunch today.

Madge and Henry were taking a boat out on the broads – the first time they had ventured

out onto water on their own. Wilfrid didn’t mind where he went as long as he was with

them.  He was a very insecure puppy and constantly worried about being left on his own.

He often confided in Fillip, “Wilfy fwightened, “he would say, “Of what now!” Fillip

would ask resigning himself to building up Wilf’s confidence get again.  Wilfrid looked

at the activity going on around him as Madge and Henry prepared for their special day.

“Madge and Henry are going somewhere and Wilfie might be left behind, and get very

hungry and firsty and die!”  “Wilfrid, don’t be daft!”  Fillip was a little exasperated.  Not

needing food himself he didn’t understand Wilfs constant worry about it.  “They haven’t

left you anywhere yet and there’s no reason to think they will!”  Wilfrid looked slyly at

Fillip.  “They left you behind!”  he said smugly.  Fillip was momentarily taken aback –

that was difficult to argue with!  Then he had an inspiration, “That was only because I

couldn’t tell them, Wilf!” he said, “whereas you can bark very loudly and run fast!

“And,” he added unkindly “they would soon miss you because there’d be a lot of food

around!”  Wilfrid was only a little comforted, he quite liked worrying – it gave him

something to do.

Fillip was popped into a hamper with the cutlery and plenty of food, through the open

spaces of the hamper he saw Wilf’s enquiring nose – “It’s  all right Wilf – there’s a tin of

dog food in here!”  Wilf relaxed – a little.  There he was picked up by the doting Madge

and ensconced in the back of the car with his cushion, a bowl of biscuits and water.  “I’ll

be alright for five minutes” thought Wilf “then what?”

The boat they were hiring was not far away and Wilf was soon out of the car again.  “Oh

dear,” he moaned “I didn’t have time to finish those biscuits,  I hope they’re there when

we come back.”  There was a deep sigh of  resignation from the hamper. Then they were

all being transferred to the boat which moved gently up and down.

“I think I’m going to be sick!” said Wilf to the hamper.  No you are not!  You wouldn’t

waste those biscuits you’ve just eaten would you?”  said the voice from the hamper so

Wilfred thought better of it and waited apprehensively to see what would happen next.

The boat trip was not a great success Madge and Henry both for once on a par for being

inexpert sailors got in each others way and Wilfrid got in everyone’s way.  Their first

problem came as they tried to pull away from the dock into open water.  Henry was keen

on watching war films, especially those about the navy so felt he had a head start on

Madge, as he watched the stern he called out to Madge who had been entrusted with the

wheel,  “Starboard Madge, turn the wheel to starboard!”  Madge peering at the sky said “I

can’t see any stars Henry, the suns too bright!”  Henry misunderstood, “It’s the opposite

of port Madge!”  “But we’re still in port Henry!”

Exasperated Henry took the wheel and banished Madge to watch the stern as he eased his

way from the quayside.  There were boats on either side and it was tricky.  “How much

room have I got?” he asked Madge.  She looking across to the other bank said, “Oh

plenty Henry, at least 20 metres I should say!”  “Henry gritted his teeth again, “And on

either side?  How long before I hit something?”  Madge waited then said, “About ----

now!”  There was a grating noise.  The boat owner turned away as though in pain and

made a note to charge them for extra paintwork when they returned - if they returned!

At last they were clear and started their leisurely way down stream towards, ultimately, Yarmouth, though Henry had no intention of going that far.  Henry gave Madge a few

instructions about what he meant by port and starboard which made Madge feel dizzy.

“What’s wrong with right and left?” she asked.  Henry gave up.

At lunch time they tied up at a quiet mooring and the hamper contents were quickly set

out.  Wilfrid put on his, “I am slowly dying of starvation!” look and did quite well out of

scraps and left overs and quite a lot of bits which were neither.

The small contingent of the Kitchen Crew were enjoying the change of scenery and fresh

air but not the thoughts of ending up in the murky green water.  They did their best to

huddle together and to be fair Madge, knowing she couldn’t rush to the barn to sculpt,

more or less concentrated.  Everyone relaxed  until a motor boat went past.

Doing far more than the 4mph indicated by the sign on the bank, the motor boat,

complete with young crew, laughing uproariously zoomed past as Henry tried to keep his

boat steady.  Keith and Kath Knife slipped unseen to the deck and with the boats motion

started to slip to one side where they were soon out of sight.  Fillip waited his time and as

the boat steadied and the mild panic subsided he saw Madge look in his direction and

slipped off the seat to the deck.  The movement caught Madge’s eye and as she bent to

pick up Fillip she saw Keith and Kath and they were rescued too.  “Phew thought Fillip!

“I wish all rescues were that simple!”

Wilfrid was hanging over the side.  Madge and Henry thought it was sweet, “Oh look!”

said Madge.  “Wilfrid wants to go into the water, lets stop somewhere so he can have a

swim!”

Madge was not a good mind reader.  Wilfrid didn’t want to swim, he didn’t even want to

get his feet wet, he was concerned about that other dog looking up at him from the water!

He didn’t think there was enough food for two dogs and hoped Madge wouldn’t take pity

on this other one and invite him on board.

After a few minutes Henry pointed out a small beach like area – “We could let Wilfrid

off there” he pointed “It’s shallow and he’ll love it!”

Wilfrid couldn’t believe what they were trying to do to him – and he thought they were

his friends!  He struggled to avoid the nasty cold wet stuff.  Being bathed at home was

different, there it was warm and he could see the limit and depth of the water, here it

seemed to go on for ever and it was so deep you couldn’t see the bottom.  So Wilfrid in

sheer desperation and self preservation struggled so that Madge and Henry might as well

have gone in fully clothed and joined him.  The experiment was not repeated!  The

Kitchen Crew in the hamper had quiet hysterics as Wilf recounted to them what had

happened. They were not very sympathetic.  They spent a lot of time at the bottom of a

sink of cold washing up water when Madge forgot about them and couldn’t see why Wilf

was worried – the fact that he had lungs and could drown and they, not having lungs and

couldn’t was inexplicable to them.

After he had been dried off Wilfrid returned to his position, hanging over the side of the

boat to prevent the other dog getting aboard and stealing his food.  But now the other dog

had been joined by an enormous white thing with what looked like a very sharp nose!

Henry grabbed Wilfrid just as the swan stretched out its beak to take a bite out of him.

It’s wings flapped angrily as its prize was snatched away.  Henry was alarmed and

grabbed the nearest thing to wave at the swan.  It happened to be Colonel Bread.  He did

his best to look fierce and hoped fervently Henry would not drop him into the dark water

to be lost from sight for ever.  But this was Henry not Madge.  Colonel Bread was

returned to the hamper, Wilfrid was tied up in the middle of the boat and they started to

make their way back the way they had come.  It was a lot easier now, though nosing into

the space at the docks was tricky; they made it without any more scrapes.

Henry stepping off the boat with Madge, Wilfrid and the hamper all safe and complete

was inclined to view the day as a triumph.  “I think I really have a feel for boating!” he

told Madge.  “Yes, I think we should make a habit of this!”  Madge, Wilfrid and the

Kitchen Crew looked apprehensive.  Henry might have enjoyed himself but they all had

reservations.  “Yes dear,” said Madge supportively but with her fingers firmly crossed

behind her back.

title: Back to the Kitchen
body:
Everyone wanted to be first to tell what had happened while the Kitchen Crew had been

separated.  But eventually they were all more or less clear on what had gone on.

“So, let me see,” said Fillip (with an F) “While we were away Tommy was lost in a dark

place and rescued by Topsy – Major Fruit and Veg jumped on the freezer, - Tommy

pulled Ted out of a hole and you were left outside – is that right?”

“Eh no, not really,” said Butts, “That’s a bit of a jumble really – it must be the way I tell

them!  But it sounds as if you had an exciting time!  Did you say Colonel Bread was left

behind and picked up by Roland the Rolling Pin, - Kneil Knife went swimming and

Wilfrid stopped the tent blowing away?”

“Not exactly,” said Fillip.  “I think we’d better start again, but not at the moment,” he

added hastily.  He could see this exchange of stories going on all day and getting more

and more muddled.

The kitchen door opened and the Kitchen Crew fell silent and immobile.  Madge walked

in with Wilfrid.  She let him out of the back door and settled down at the table with a cup

of coffee and the newspaper.

The phone rang and Madge was delighted to hear Fiona at the other end.  After some

general conversation catching up on Madge’s caravan holiday – an expurgated version

from Madge about her sailing ability – and Fiona’s recent trip to Germany, Fiona wanted

to make arrangements to meet Madge in London but wasn’t sure when she would be free.

“Mum, I’ll ring you back as soon as I know the time we can meet, so don’t go out till I’ve

phoned you back – OK?”

Madge agreed though it made her feel a bit tied down.  Henry was out and as there was

no phone in the barn she couldn’t go and sculpt while she waited.  She had the brilliant

idea of clearing and sorting out the pantry and was soon immersed in its depths, flinging

out empty boxes and containers willy-nilly.

Wilfrid barged back in thru’ the half open back door, (he’d already tried the cat flap, got

stuck and had to be rescued by Henry).  Crumpet had been amused and scornful, he

didn’t like to share anything – it was a bit annoying that the Kitchen Crew had managed

to use the flap to rescue Tommy.  He would have to see that didn’t happen again!  Wilfrid

flounced around the kitchen, shaking off raindrops and leaves from his long, black silky

fur.  During one of these flings he managed to get tangled up in the telephone cable, just

enough to break contact.  It looked OK but would not ring if Fiona or anyone else called.

The Kitchen Crew were alerted when after a few minutes a mechanical voice came from

it and said, to anyone who happened to be listening, “Please hang up and try again,” –

eight times.   Madge deep in the cupboard of course did not hear. Even when she

emerged she did not notice. Leaving the back door open, so she could hear the phone (as

she thought) she started moving all the rubbish she had cleared out to the dustbin.  Once

out there she felt like doing a bit of gardening, still convinced she would hear the phone if

it rang.

The Kitchen Crew was undecided what to do.  The call had sounded urgent and Madge

was pinned down waiting for it, but they didn’t usually show their liveliness when

someone was so close.

Fillip called Wilfrid over and explained that because of his clumsiness Madge was

waiting for a call which would not come!  Wilf was crestfallen.  “Sowy Fillip, Wilfy bad

boy!  How can I help?”  We have to be careful not to be seen to be enlivened Wilf,”

stated Fillip “I think we can put things right fairly easily but we must be sure Madge

doesn’t see us.  You are going to have to watch her in the garden and warn us if you think

she’s going to walk in on us – can you do that?”

“Eh, I fink so” said Wilf dubiously, he had only half followed what Fillip said, something

about it being his fault that Madge was tied up?  He looked out of the kitchen door and

was puzzled.  Madge was walking round the garden and didn’t seem to be at all tied up.

He went back to Fillip who was conferring with Butts about the phone problem.

“Eh, ‘scuse me Fwip,” Wilf began humbly.  “Madge is OK, not tied up at all!”  Fillip

sighted heavily, really it was a bit much that a fork had more brain cells than a dog! Then

he reminded himself Wilf was still a pup and had a lot still to learn.  He quietly and

patiently explained the problem and the plan to Wilf- trying to use words of one syllable.

Light dawned in Wilfrid’s dark sad eyes and he prepared to do his part in the PLAN.  He

stationed himself next to the back door and watched Madge intently.  It would be alright

as long as he didn’t fall asleep – being a puppy was very tiring.

Fillip and Butts climbed onto the table and looked at the new silent phone.  “It’s only a

little bit out of place,” said Butts, “but still its enough to stop it ringing and that will be

inconvenient for Madge and Fiona will think her mother is being scatty again, she won’t

realise its Wilf’s fault.  They looked across at the door where Wilf was on watch, his

head was sinking slowly and he looked as though he would be in the land of nod before

too long.

“This ‘phone is too heavy for us to move,” said Fillip “We need help.”  He looked across

at Wilf again, “And I think our guard dog needs help too!”

“Tommy Teaspoon – do you want to help in another rescue?”

Tommy could hardly climb out of his drawer in his excitement.

“Yes sir, anything sir, what can I do sir?”

Fillip was a bit embarrassed at Tommy’s obvious hero worship.  “There’s no need for all

that, sonny!” he said, “All we want you to do is keep Wilfrid company and wake him up

if he falls asleep!  Tommy could talk a blue streak so it was no problem to regale Wilfrid

with the stories of the Kitchen Crew and Wilfrid was grateful for the company.  So Fillip

and Butts went back to concocting yet another rescue plan to get Madge out of trouble.

“What we need is someone who can give the handset a good shove,” said Butts, “Who

can we ask?  It would be nice to involve someone who hasn’t been asked before. It’s not

really a big job – how about Roland?  All he had done is to let himself be used as a mallet

– it was painful and a bit humiliating!”

So Roland was called out of his slumber drawer and was quite willing to help. Wilfrid

barked loudly, everyone resumed their normal inanimate forms and waited.  Nothing

happened.

“Sowy!” said Wilf “False alarm – she did look as though she was coming in!”

“Never mind,” said Fillip “At least it shows you we’re ready and you did the right thing.

Now Roland, lets get you in position.”

Roland lined up at the end of the ‘phone and swung his little foot at it.  The first time, he

fell over with a crash!  He was quite heavy.  But Madge didn’t hear and they started

again.

“Here,” said Butts, “Fillip and I will stand on either side of you to support you, maybe

that will help your balance!”

Roland a bit shamefaced at his mistake was desperate to do the job properly – after all

they might ask Ted Tenderiser next time;  he and Roland were always arguing about who

was biggest and strongest.  He took careful aim and with Fillip and Butts holding on to

him tightly he kicked out at the ‘phone.  But just as a he was about to connect, the cat flap

clanged noisily behind Crumpet and Roland missed completely. All three fell over in a

heap, much to Crumpet’s amusement, it was about time he had his own back on them,

“Oops!” he said “sorry about that, if I’d only known – ha ha ha!”

Wilfrid was distracted too, he loved to greet Crumpet with a long wet tongue.  Crumpet

hated it and batted him, none too gently, with his paw, claws out.  The Kitchen Crew

heard the claws peeling off Wilfrid’s face and winced.  Wilf didn’t mind.  Crumpet had

noticed him and given him one of those nice prickly pats.  With difficulty he was sent

back to his post and Roland prepared to have another go.  This time it was successful and

the phone rang almost immediately.  Wilf barked as Madge hurried in and the Kitchen

Crew once again played the dead game.  The call WAS important, Madge left almost

straight away to meet Fiona, vowing to contact the ‘phone company because Fiona hadn’t

been able to get through.  The Kitchen Crew heaved a collective sigh of relief and

relaxed.  Life was never dull now they had been enlivened.

title: The Sick Fish
body:
Madge was a generous person, generous with money, love and time.  She found it

difficult to say no to any plea for help.  (Maybe this was why the Kitchen Crew was so

helpful, they caught the bug from her!)  So when the neighbour who willingly fed

Crumpet asked her to look after her small fish tank Madge was only too glad to agree.

Though she couldn’t think why a fish tank needed looking after!  Maybe it was valuable?

Her neighbour, Pat, arrived with the tank.  At once Madge realised she had been naïve, it

wasn’t just a tank, it was a tank with fish in it.  The tank was heavy so it was wheeled

into the kitchen on a trolley.  Madge had decided the kitchen was the best place, when

she was told about all the feeding arrangements, she was less likely to forget all about

them if she was feeding Henry, Wilf and herself at the same time. So Flash and Flush

arrived together with an awful lot of paraphernalia for two small shubunkins.  Their

luggage included several books on fish care as well as their food and a range of

medicines in case they should go down with any one of many nasty diseases to which fish

are prone.

The Kitchen Crew were intrigued and gathered round to watch the aimless wanderings of

Flash and Flush.  Wilfrid was interested too, “Where’s their food bowl?” he asked Fillip.

“They don’t need one,” he was told, “they get a pinch of powdery stuff now and again –

ants eggs,” Wilfrid was not impressed, “that wouldn’t suit me!” he said, “Wilfy would be

hungry all day – they don’t have a water bowl either!”  Fillip looked at him to see if he

was joking.  He wasn’t!

Wilma the Whisk was nostalgic.  “Gee, that takes me back,” she said, “They had a big

tank of fish in my hardware store!  It was supposed to calm the customers down so they

stayed longer and spent more bucks there!  They were cute little fellows – a lovely gold

colour – not patchy like these creatures!”  “They’re patchy because they’re shubunkins.”

said Fillip “they’re supposed to be like that!”  The fish themselves didn’t make much

contribution to the conversation.  It was quite hard to communicate through the water and

the heavy glass. None of the Kitchen Crew were very good at lip reading but they did

their best, just to be friendly.  Butts looked hard and then said, “I think Flash said he was

cold, but it could have been ‘bold’ or even ‘gold’ – except he’s not gold or only patchy

gold anyway.”  It was not much use trying to talk to them, Fillip remembered hearing a

goldfish’s memory was only seven seconds long, “So, they wouldn’t remember what we

said anyway!” he said. The Kitchen Crew gradually lost interest and went back to their

quiet uneventful lives.

Madge was very good, she remembered to feed the fish but even she didn’t seem to get

much pleasure out of talking to them and concentrated on Wilf and Crumpet and of

course the Kitchen Crew.  Crumpet was very interested in the fish and had to be forcibly

restrained from adding them to his menu.  A tea tray was put on top of the tank to thwart

further fishing expeditions.

Madge got so used to the fish being there.  She would feed them with hardly a glance so

she did not notice when their habits changed. She did not notice that they seemed to be

swimming more slowly and stayed nearer the bottom.  It was Fillip who first wondered if

something was wrong.  He went close to the glass, it wasn’t easy climbing up the side of

the trolley, Flash (or maybe Flush) came to the glass next to him and seemed to be

mouthing at him but couldn’t make head or tail of anything they might be trying to say.

The fish had been in residence for a few days and the kitchen Crew had got used to their

boring little ways and lack of communication. They paid almost as little attention to them

as Madge.  The exception was Wilfrid, he was often shut in the kitchen when Henry was

at work and Madge was sculpting and he was bored.  He had tried his best to get Crumpet

to play with him but the supercilious cat looked down his nose at the pup.  “No, I don’t

want to play with you – you smell!” Wilfrid was hurt.  “Wilfy don’t smell bad, Wilfie

smell like dog!” he argued “Exactly!” said Crumpet “I rest my case!” – he would stalk

away with his nose in the air to enjoy his own company.

So Wilfrid took to watching the fish.  Round and round they went, day in and day out, it

was very soothing and he often fell asleep.  Then one day Fillip was alerted because he

was almost sure he had heard Wilfrid laughing.  At least he was looking at the fish and

wagging his tail and woofing gently.

“What are you doing Wilf?” asked Fillip intrigued at this change of behaviour.  “Fish

doing twicks for Wilfy!” said the pup.  Fish do’nt really  do twicks  – er tricks Wilf” he

said, “What do you mean?”

“Look” said Wilf and Fillip watched as Flush (or maybe Flash) floated to the top of the

tank and started swimming upside down. Wilf laughed, “See” he said, “they do twicks for

Wilfs, fish funny!”

Fillip wasn’t convinced, something was wrong but he didn’t know what!  He didn’t know

what to do or who to ask.  Wilma was not much help.  “Sure, honey, they do look kinda

funny, but it’s not something I’ve ever seen before.  The fish in my hardware store didn’t

behave like that, they were just regular little guys!”  Butts was no help either, he didn’t

know anything about fish, “But!” he said with sudden inspiration “I bet I know someone

who does!”

At the back of a drawer was a very shy, lonely member of the Kitchen Crew. He was not

related to anyone, being neither knife, nor folk, nor spoon.  His nearest relative was Peter

Pallet but they did not have much to say to each other!  This was Fred Fish Slice.  Butts

crawled to the back of the drawer where Fred was known to lurk.  “We need your expert

help Fred!” said Butts.  “Who me?” said Fred lugubriously  “I’m not an expert at

anything – know nothing – that’s me, my middle name is Ignoramus!”  “Oh come on

Fred!” said Butts jovially, trying to inject some confidence and enthusiasm into poor old

Fred.  “We’ve got a question about fish – you’re the only one who is likely to know about

them!”  Fred brightened “Fried or poached?” he asked, “Battered or bread crumbed?”

Butts heart sank “No not cooked fish Fred! Live ones, ones swimming around but – well

acting strangely!”  Fred was intrigued in spite of himself  “Well, I’ll have a look, but I

don’t think I can help, don’t expect too much!”  “Just have a look at them Fred!”

So Fred made his way over to the tank, feeling more important than usual.  He envied the

Kitchen Crew exploits and though he could have joined in knowing they would welcome

him, he was very shy.

He climbed onto a unit nearest the fish tank and watched them carefully.  He frowned as

he saw them swimming lopsidedly and sometimes near the top upside down!  He racked

his little brain, “It looks familiar,” he said, “let me see …….. of course!  They probably

have swim bladder disease!”

“What on earth is that?” said Fillip “I don’t really know, it’s just something fish get and

have to be treated for!”

“Well we can’t treat them!” said Butts.  “There are medicines which came with them but

we can’t open the bottles and we might give too much or too little!  We have to alert

Madge and hope she’ll do something.

After a lot of half baked ideas were bandied around there was only one feasible one and it

was very risky.  Someone would have to slip into the tank so Madge would notice. It was

risky because Madge might seriously wonder what was going on!  Hopefully she would

blame Crumpet and he would have to take his chances!

Fillip volunteered to be the one to slip into the murky fish tank.  It wasn’t much different

from being in dirty washing up water he assured the others and he couldn’t drown.  The

only problem was shifting the Crumpet proof tea tray Madge had put on top.  As it

happened it was easy enough as she had not put it back on quite straight the last feeding

time.   So Fillip waited until Madge was due to make breakfast.  He heard her in the hall

and slipped in.  It was a while before Madge did notice but at last she did and frowned

when she saw the erratic behaviour of Flash and Flush.  She knew nothing about fish so

looking up the symptoms in one of the books which the fish had brought with them, she

identified the symptoms and sure enough it was swim bladder disease and one of the

medicines brought in by her friend was suitable.  The fish recovered and Madge was

spared the trauma of two funerals and an explanation!  Fred was feted for his expertise

and welcomed to the kitchen Crew as a full member and gave up languishing in the back

of the drawer..

title: From Sick fish to Sick Plant!
body:
Madge loved her garden.  It was no wonder the housework suffered as she spent any time

available from sculpting growing flowers, fruit and vegetables.  Madge was not exactly a

GREAT gardener, she was too absent minded for that.  This meant that sometimes she

deadheaded new buds, realising too late that they weren’t shrivelled enough to warrant

culling.  She also had a certain lack of success when she tried to grow things out of

season.  Spring bulbs planted in spring do not flower in summer, she found to her

surprise.  As well as the garden Madge loved houseplants, though she had less success

here.  Outside at least the plants could rely on being rained on occasionally and too much

rain would drain away.  Inside it was another matter.  Madge was erratic in her watering,

tending to notice the need only when faced with wilting, dried up leaves, then she tried to

make amends by over watering.   Many plants could not take this switching back and

forth between drought and flood and gave up trying.  Some succeeded in spite of Madge.

Usually her plants were kept in the living room so the Kitchen Crew did not see much of

them – just the ones which had given up and which Madge sadly deposited in the rubbish

bag.

One day Madge brought home a plant given her by a friend. It was rather large for its pot

so she decided to repot it.  She had, after all plenty of spare pots of varying sizes and

plenty of soil in the garden.  Furthermore she decided to keep the pot in the kitchen where

she could keep an eye on it and water it when needed.  (Madge often decided to water the

plants in the living room but on her way to the kitchen for water would forget where she

was going and why).  It was a sturdy little plant and looked a lot more comfortable in its

big pot.  The Kitchen Crew were mildly interested at first but when Fillip failed to

enliven it they lost interest.  Wilfrid decided with surprise that it wasn’t edible and left it

alone.

At first Madge was very solicitous of its welfare but as the days passed and it just sat

there doing nothing in particular she too lost interest but remembered to water it fairly

often, if not regularly.

It was Fillip (of course) who being at a loose end one day climbed onto the unit where the

little plant sat and with nothing better to do inspected it thoroughly.  It looked a bit odd,

he thought, not as well and thriving as it had done when it arrived, in fact it seemed to be

a bit smaller, as though it was shrinking.

He climbed up the side of the pot with difficulty and examined the earth.  Nothing odd

there, it was not too wet or too dry. But as he looked Fillip thought he detected a

movement.  It wasn’t the plant, he had proved it couldn’t be enlivened so there must be

something else in the pot!  Gingerly he used his prongs to feel about in the earth and felt

something squishy and slimy.  He recoiled in disgust. Then, ready this time, he had

another feel around.  He moved the earth away from the plant  stem and piled it up

around the edge of the pot.  Then he saw it  “A large, fat shiny creature had made its

home in the plant roots and was slowly sucking it dry.  “No wonder it looked as if  it was

shrinking!” thought Fillip.  “That thing, whatever it is, is slowly killing it.  I wonder if

this is something the Kitchen Crew can tackle, Madge won’t notice it until too late!”

Fillip went off to find Butts and ask his advice;  Butts shinned up the unit and had a look

for himself.  “I think its called a caterpillar,” he said “Something laid eggs there, a moth I

expect and now the caterpillar is feeding on the plant roots until its big enough to change

into a larva and then a moth.  But by that time the little plant will have died!”  “What can

we do to save it?”  Wondered Fillip.  “It’s right in the middle of the roots, I can’t dig it

out without damaging it!”

Help came from an unexpected quarter.  The wooden spoon girls had heard Fillip and

Butts talking and had some answers!  “It’s because we’re wood, you see,” said Olive.

“We used to grow in the ground because we were once part of a tree.  You’ll have to get

the plant out of the pot, check the soil for more grubs and get rid of them before putting

the plant back.” “That sound like a big job for us.” said Fillip doubtfully, “We’ll need a

lot of help!”

The wooden spoon girls put their heads together and surprisingly had some good ideas.

“Ask Sophie Sieve if you can put the plant in her when you and some other forks have

dug it out – then get her to swish around in that soapy water Madge has left in the sink –

the grub won’t be able to hold on to the roots.”

Holly chipped in, “Then look through the soil for other grubs and eggs and get them out –

you forks will be good at that!”

So the mammoth job started.  Fillip and Fergus dug away until they were breathless and

the plant was free of the soil.  Sophie was ready so they tipped the plant into her and

helped her down into the sink.  Olive was right, the grub didn’t like the soapy water and

let go of the roots to try and get away from it.

“Now!” said Butts quickly, “Get Sophie out and rescue the plant before the grub realises

what’s happening!”

Florence and Flora had finished digging around in the soil and found a couple of eggs

which they tipped out.  With a lot of heaving and pushing Fillip and Fergus got the plant

back into its pot but it was quite loose and floppy.

“Can we help Fillip?” Stuart and Sadie Spoons wanted to be join in  too.  They soon got

busy spooning the soil back and pressing it down firmly around the little plant.

There only remained the job of clearing up the mess so Madge would not suspect what

had happened.  They didn’t know how to get rid of the soil on the unit; they had no

access to a brush and pan.  They couldn’t ask Wilfrid to help because he couldn’t reach

the unit.  He was sitting looking up at them wishing he could join in but it was out of the

question.  Asking Crumpet to help was laughable though he could have made short work

of it with his furry paws and the Kitchen Crew, though they thought as hard as they

could, could not come up with a plan to trick him into helping this time.

So with a heavy sigh Wilma offered her service.  “It’s not something I like to do too

often,” she said, “but my tines could be used to sweep the bits of soil into something, it

wouldn’t be perfect but it would be better than nothing.  But where shall I sweep them?”

They looked around, perplexed.  They really had to clear up or Madge would certainly

suspect something, even she would remember if she’d left soil on the units.

“Down there, a bit further along – on the floor, see it?” that was the resourceful Fillip.

They looked; as usual Madge had left a rubbish bag, full but not tied up waiting to be put

out.  Wilfrid had looked at it longingly but experience had taught him at last that it wasn’t

worth the smack when he got all the scraps anyway and had had only a mouthful of

pealings last time.  He shuddered as he remembered the awful task.

So Wilma set about brushing the soil the few centimetres along the unit top then all the

Kitchen Crew took it in turns to scoop or kick bits over the edge.  At last it was more or

less clear though there were still some marks left.  “I can clear those, with my big flat

blade!” said Peter Pallet shyly and set about clearing away the last signs of the Kitchen

Crew’s latest activity.

Madge came back, noticed nothing, but tutted as she wiped over the work surface.

Humming as she worked she tied up the bag and took it out then came back and praised

Wilf for being a big boy and leaving the bag alone.  “Huh,” thought Wilf, “even I’m not

that daft!”  The grub in the sink disappeared down the plug hole and was never seen

again.

Gradually the little plant revived and started to do quite well even putting out little red

flowers.  Madge was pleased and congratulated herself on her gardening abilities.

“Look” she said to Fillip as she washed him.” Aren’t I a clever little gardener Mr Fork!”

Fillip signed in resignation and looked for another problem to solve.

title: Jimmy in Danger Again!
body:
Flash and Flush’s owners seemed to have gone away for a very long time thought Fillip,

but at least Madge had been able to treat them for their swim bladder disease and they

were swimming normally again.  Fillip decided not to relax this time as when he did

something happened, but then that was inevitable when you lived in a house with Madge.

So when little Jimmy put his head around the kitchen door and chuckled, Fillip was ready

and watchful.  The Kitchen Crew put on their usual little show for him, safe because he

could not yet speak and give the game away. Jimmy watched as the Kitchen Crew

cavorted around, pretending to fall off the work surface and then climbing back up via the

drawer handles.  The knives and forks kept themselves carefully hidden away and Claude

especially crawled to the back of his drawer.  So with only  Sophie Sieve, the spoons and

Peter Pallet to entertain him Jimmy got bored and looked around for something else with

which to occupy himself.  He knew he didn’t have much time before Madge and his

mother missed him and came looking for him.  The kitchen was by far the most

interesting room in the house and he was sure he would find something to play with soon.

Fillip watched him from a vantage point near the cooker where he could not be  reached

by little enquiring fingers, he was aware his prongs would be quite sharp in small

inexpert hands.

Wilfrid lollopped around Jimmy glad to have someone to play with but Jimmy had a dog

at home so Wifrid was not much of a distraction and after rolling him over a couple of

times he looked around again for something new.

He watched the fish for a while but they ignored him, more interested in the castle and

treasure chest at the bottom of the tank.  Jimmy tried to reach into the tank to see how wet

the water was but it was too high up f or him.  Then he spotted the tubs of fish food and

medicines next to the table.  He picked up one tub and shook it, it rattled. That was quite

interesting.  Another tub had a softer sound, a sort of powdery shaking.  The tubs were

small and portable and Jimmy soon had them arranged on the floor around him.  Fillip

and Butts watched anxiously.  They knew they were not going to be easy to open because

even Madge had had trouble and had muttered something about child proof tops being

too difficult for even adults to open.  So being one to cut corners and save time if possible

Madge had left one lid half undone- the one with the fish disease medicine, the one that

rattled interestingly when Jimmy shook it.  “Sweetie!” said Jimmy and made short work

of the insecurely fastened top.

He peered inside at the brownish tablets and he wasn’t sure – they smelled funny and

didn’t look much like any sweets he’d ever been given but if the proof of the pudding

was in the eating so must the proof of the sweeties be!

His chubby fingers had trouble reaching into the pot but eventually he got one out and

looked at it. As he slowly raised it to his mouth he was thumped in the back by Wilfrid.

Wilfrid was acting under instructions from an anxious Butts and Fillip, watching

horrified from the counter but unsure what they could do.  “Wilf, you must stop Timmy

eating those pills AT ALL COSTS!  They will make him ill, or worse!”  “Awright Fiwip,

I’ll do my bestest.”  So Wilfrid gently thumped Timmy in the back and tried to get him to

play, “Woof!” he said encouragingly, “Woof, woof, woof!”  Jimmy was distracted

momentarily and rolled onto his back so Wilfrid could lick him.  But even this

entertainment palled eventually, “Where on earth is his mother – and where is Madge?”

Fillip paced up and down on the counter then spied the two women in the garden

admiring Madge’s horticultural prowess.

“We’re going to have to do something, Butts, Wilf isn’t going to keep him occupied for

long, what can we do?”

They were interrupted by the cat flap clang as Crumpet arrived as noisily as usual.  He

looked around and took in the situation.  His kitchen, now not only full of smelly dog but

full of small noisy child as well.  He was disgusted, his once peaceful life seemed a long

time ago.  He put up with Wilf’s welcoming licks and clawed his face, fairly gently.  He

was beginning to appreciate that though smelly and given to big wet licks at least he was

always glad to see him, unlike the Kitchen Crew who usually became very quiet and

watchful when he arrived.  It was getting harder to find one of them on the floor which he

could play with before batting them under the units.  Last time he had had a go at Fillip

and for some reason the Butter Knife had fallen on him from the counter, it had not hurt

much but made him jump and lose his dignity for a moment.  At least the Kitchen Crew

had learned not to bother him with pleas for help in one of their mad schemes.

So he was surprised to be addressed by Fillip. “Look Crumpet I know it’s asking a lot and

you don’t like us and all that, but could you just for once help?  Look at Jimmy, he’s

playing with fish tablets and if he eats one he could be ill, please do something!”

Crumpet opened his mouth to sneer and refuse then something stopped him.  Wilfrid and

several of the Kitchen Crew were looking at him expectantly and maybe he was the only

one who could save the day!  (not to mention Jimmy).  He thought back to how he had

refused to help in the past, and how they had had to trick him into doing things to aid

their rescue plans.  For a moment he was as ashamed as a cat can be – liked not very

much, just a smidgeon,  but what he could do by helping now was to restore some respect

for him amongst the Kitchen Crew. Why – they might even regard him as a bit of a hero!

He had visions of newspaper headlines, “Crumpet saves child from horrible death!”

“Crumpet, the intelligent cat praised.”  “Crumpet receives medal from mayor.”

“All right, just this once mind, what do you want me to do?”  Fillip and Butts looked at

each other, amazed and hastily revised their opinion of Crumpet as selfish, egotistical and

unhelpful.

“Could you get that pill away from Jimmy?  And somehow stop him eating any?”

Wilfrid thumped Jimmy playfully again and the toddler dropped the pill.  As he reached

out a chubby hand to retrieve it he was beaten to it by a furry paw.  Crumpet front paws

flailing, butted the pill away from Jjimmy and sent it skidding under the cooker.  The

toddler was outraged, with difficulty he pulled another pill from the tub.  Once again he

was thumped by Wilf, dropped the pill and watched in disbelief as Crumpet batted it

away, it also went under the cooker.

Crumpet was beginning to enjoy himself.  Fancy him, a cat, co-operating with Wilf, a

dog and doing a good deed all at once.  Crumpet’s self esteem reasserted itself and he

started to stride around.  But Jimmy was not to be thwarted – those sweeties must be very

good indeed if everyone was determined to stop him having one. So he cut corners and

instead of picking out one pill with difficulty he tipped the pot up and spilled the contents

onto the floor.  Now he had a choice, Crumpet couldn’t get them all away from him.  The

cat stopped his celebratory stalk around the kitchen and hastily batted the nearest pill

under the fridge.  But Jimmy was chuckling now there was another pill he could go for!

Fillip and Butts realised Crumpet couldn’t cope – “Come on,” said Fillip we can do what

Crumpet’s doing!”  So down they jumped and with their little feet and feeble kicks got

two more pills out of harm’s way.  Jimmy was in his element, it had become a game now

to beat this lot of interfering busybodies and get at least one ‘sweetie’ into his mouth. But

co-operation was the Kitchen Crew’s middle name, before Jimmy’s astonished gaze,

several spoons joined in and they too kicked at the pills until they had all disappeared.

Jimmy burst into tears.  At that moment his mum and Madge appeared, took in the scene

and jumped to the wrong conclusion as usual.  “Quick,” said Madge, “Get him into the

car and we’ll take him to the hospital!”  Somehow Jimmy never again felt the need to

investigate strange pills!  The Kitchen Crew congratulated each other and made a fuss of

Crumpet which he quite enjoyed.  Then just for the fun of it he batted Fillip under the

cooker too!.

title: Timber!
body:
The Kitchen Crew was bored and frustrated.  They couldn’t romp around the kitchen as

soon as Madge and Henry were out of sight because there were workman in  and they

seemed to spend most of their time in the kitchen, either making tea or sitting around the

table drinking it. Eventually the renovation work being done moved into the kitchen itself

which meant that from 9 am – 5 pm there was something going on there and the Kitchen

Crew exploits were confined to the hours of darkness.

The workmen were a messy lot.  Every evening when they went home they seemed to

leave tools and bits of wood around and the Kitchen Crew became used to falling over

things when they played around but as far as they could tell there was nothing

particularly dangerous that they felt the need to deal with.  Madge was used to a mess

anyway and had developed a habit of stepping daintily over anything in her way.   Many

of the Kitchen Crew were not really vulnerable but Wilma the Whisk and Sophie Sieve

were very glad not to be flattened by Madge when they sometimes found themselves left

on the floor.

The workmen had been given a key to the back door so they didn’t have to trail cement

and plaster through the house.  Crumpet stayed out of the way voluntarily, being happy

for once to be fed outside in the garden.  Wilf was kept out of the way perforce.  He was

a nosy dog and would have loved to get stuck in and investigate everything. So the

Kitchen Crew did not even have the amusement afforded by the antics of Crumpet and

Wilfrid.

On Friday afternoon the men left earlier than usual.  Henry was going to have a really

good time clearing up tonight!  As well as Madge’s chaos – somehow she had managed

to use the kitchen in spite of the new occupants – there were bags of cement stacked

around the walls and plaster and planks of wood leaning at crazy angles everywhere.  It

was one plank of wood in particular which was bothering Fillip  from his position leaning

out of the top drawer – left open as usual by Madge, (the Kitchen Crew had no shortage

of fresh air) he surveyed the scene.  The bags of cement and plaster were not a problem,

they sagged wearily against the wall and weren’t going anywhere.  The planks were

another matter; or rather one plank in particular was exercising Fillip’s little brain.  The

more he looked at it the more worried he got.  It leaned against the wall next to the door;

it wasn’t very well balanced, leaning towards the door at a crazy angle.  Alone it might

have slid slowly towards someone opening the door with a flourish (which Henry tended

to do on a Friday) a quick witted man like Henry, would see it coming and deftly catch it

before it brained him.  However lying on the floor between this long plank and the door

was a shorter almost insignificant piece of wood.  It didn’t matter that Fillip’s knowledge

of geometry and physics was non existent, he could see that the door opening, with

Henry’s flourish, would move the piece of wood sharply towards the plank, giving it

quite a whack at the base, the plank would fall very heavily towards the person coming

in.  Henry would be hurt, maybe quite badly.

Fillip called Butts and pointed out the situation to him and asked his opinion. Butts took

it all in and had to agree with Fillip that something needed to be done.  But what?

Crumpet was out looking for his weekly shrew offering and Wilfrid had been banned

from the kitchen for the duration of the renovations.  The Kitchen Crew was on its own.

“Maybe we can make the plank fall before Henry comes in?”  that was Butts suggestion

and though they all agreed no one could think how to do it.  They all tried kicking the

piece of wood but once again their puny strength was insufficient to have any effect.  The

Kitchen Crew was stumped.

“The next best think to making the plank fall before Henry comes in,” said Butts slowly,

“would be to stop him coming in at all!”  Fillip frowned, “He has to come in sometime,”

he objected , “the kitchen is the main room in the house, they couldn’t  not come

indefinitely!”

“You’re right, of course,” said Butts morosely.  “I think the Kitchen Crew is beaten this

time!  We’ll just have to hope it doesn’t happen!”  There was a creaking sound and the

dangerous plank moved a little nearer to the door, it was now very precarious indeed!

Fillip was thinking aloud, “What would happen if Henry simply couldn’t come in at all?”

he mused.

“Why honey, that’s no problem!” Wilma was putting her brain to work too, “he’d go

round to the yard and come in the back door.  The workmen have a key but it’s not the

only one!”

“The yard – oh, of course, Wilma, you mean the garden!  That’s right, we have to stop

him coming in the normal way!”

There was silence again, it was only half of a good idea, they could hardly shout through

the door to Henry to go the other way!  They all balked at giving away their secret

liveliness!

Wilma was in full flood now.  She still felt a bit competitive being a ‘Yank’ (as  she put

it) and wanted to ‘keep her end up’ and ‘fly the flag’!  Some of which expressions baffled

the Kitchen Crew.  But she had long since been accepted by them and they listened to her

now.

“What we need to do, guys as you say is to stop Henry coming in that door at all and the

only way we can do that is to stop it opening!”

“How Wilma?”  Fillip was patient “Will you jump up and turn the key or shall I?”

“Now sonny, don’t you try and be cute with me!  You know we can’t do that!  But we

might be able to stop the door opening another way!”

Wilma was toying with them!  She knew she had the answer but she wanted to make the

most of her moment of glory.  Try as they might they could not fathom what she meant

and one by one they gave in and begged her to reveal her master plan.

“Look over there” she drawled, “what do you see?”

“More of the workmen’s mess,” said Butts in disgust, he was a tidy soul and loved to

spread butter to the edge of the bread and not a millimetre further!

“Ah ha,” said Wilma, “but what a useful mess!”

“Wilma!” said Fillip through gritted teeth, “if you don’t tell us soon, I’ll …. I’ll….. do

something to your …… your whiskey bits!”

“OK, OK,” said Wilma hurriedly    “I’m thinking of that big cloth thing – I think they use

it to stop paint splashing around.  If we could drag that in front of the door it would not

be able to open and Henry would go to the back door!”  She was breathless with the

excitement of being the one to think this up…. How she wished she could tell the others

in the hardware store!

Fillip was cautiously optimistic,  “It has its good point Wilma, Madge and Henry

wouldn’t suspect us, its just the sort of thing those workmen would do!  The disadvantage

is …… its an awfully big cloth and we’re very little and weak.”

Wilma was ahead of him, “Now, now – don’t be pessimistic!  There are a lot of us, we

can all pull together – literally!  (She was particularly pleased with that pun!)  And we

only have to drag it far enough to slow the door down.  Just time for Henry to see what is

going to happen and stop it!”

Fillip was getting excited, “You’re on Wilma – old girl – it could work.  Right

everybody, and I mean everybody, Claude – you can be triple purpose for once!”  Claude,

who had been looking down his long aristocratic nose at all this talk of pulling together

(HIM and THAT LOT, Tiens, non, non, non) immediately changed his mind and

prepared to join in.

And so one of the strangest sights the kitchen had ever seenn began.  All the knives, forks

and spoons, all the cooking and serving tools climbed down and with their tiny hands

took an edge of the heavy dust sheet and started to tug.  It was had work – Fred Fish Slice

had the bright idea of spurring everyone on with a sea shanty, “Yo heave ho!” he

yodelled, “Yo, heave ho!”  It seemed to work, it made them all laugh and start to think of

the job as fun, not just hard work.  Little by little they were winning and just before the

last teaspoon fell exhausted, AND just before they heard Henry’s footfall, the cloth was

in position.  Hastily they scrambled back to where Madge had left them.  Henry couldn’t

open the door!  They heard him grumbling and a few minutes later in he came through

the back door.  He saw the cloth and, muttering in annoyance, moved it out of the way

and tidied the wood and the plank away too.  He didn’t even realise from what he had

been saved!  “Never mind!” thought Wilma, “the Kitchen Crew know who saved his

bacon, and that will have to be enough!”

title: Poor Old Crumpet!
body:
The war between Crumpet and the Kitchen Crew was a cold one.  It was more a lack of

co-operation on Crumpet’s part and for the Kitchen Crew they regretted that they did not

have him as an ally since he could do things they couldn’t.  For one thing he was quite

strong, they envied him his muscles and the other thing they would have liked to use was

his ability to come and go through his cat flap.  They had tricked him several times into

helping them and he was becoming more and more wary – not to mention paranoid.

Whereas he had popped in and out of his cat flap without a second thought, now he

would put his head through cautiously, look around and then step in gingerly.  It was not

that he expected to be attacked; he knew the Kitchen Crew was not like that.  But he

didn’t like his good nature (his WHAT?) to be taken advantage of. If he was going to

help it would be on HIS terms, though he could never see that happening in a month of

Sundays!

But one day the tables were turned!  Madge had begun to feed Crumpet when the ‘phone

in the hall rang.  So his tin was left on the unit, top taken off but not scooped out into his

dish, Crumpet was peeved.  He liked routine and though he knew Madge would come

back eventually he would have to wait, who knew how long and he WAS hungry.  So he

started to feed himself, he could use his paw to scoop out some food from his tin but it

was a slow, tedious business and he didn’t like the feel of meat between his toes (well,

would you?) and he had to have a thorough wash every now and again.  Madge was a

long time and in a moment of inattention Crumpet moved the tin too close to the edge of

the unit and had to watch in horror as it disappeared onto the floor.  It landed on its side

and for a while Crumpet continued to be able to eat though awkwardly.

The Kitchen Crew watched, slightly amused at Crumpet the Elegant’s clumsiness.  But a

sound in the hall made them look at each other with some alarm.  Wilfrid was coming!

He would do his best to eat Crumpet’s food and if Madge didn’t notice Crumpet would

go hungry while Wilfrid would have his own meal as well.

“We’ve got to help Crumpet!” said Fillip.

“What?  Are you mad?  Help that creature when he’s never helped us?  Why?” that was

Butts sounding indignant.

“It’s part of a cunning plan!”  explained Fillip. “If we help Crumpet, he might help us in

the future – you never know, he might have a conscience!”  Butts fell off the unit

laughing and had to climb back up via the draw handles.

“Crumpet, with a conscience?  Oh quick, look, there’s a pig flying past the window!”

Fillip found himself twisting round to look before he realised Butts was being sarcastic!

“Oh alright, I know it’s unlikely but even if he never does help us, we’ll have shown him

how decent folk look out for each other!”

“I suppose you’re right!” grumbled Butts, “but what can we do against Wilf, he’s so big

and heavy he will flatten us if we stand in his way!”

“Then we’ll just have to be crafty, as usual.” said Fillip.

With that, in lumbered Wilfrid, they could hear Madge still on the ‘phone, Wilfrid would

make short work of Crumpet’s tin!  Crumpet looked up as Wilfrid came in and realised

he was about to lose his dinner to the big, smelly dog who had invaded his life.  Briefly

he thought of asking the Kitchen Crew for help but knew with a sinking heart that he had

burned his bridge there!  He saw Fillip looking at him contemplatively and squirmed as

he thought of the amusement they would get out of his predicament.

Wilfrid was thrilled!  How thoughtful of Madge to leave him a snack, she must have

known how hungry he would be after his walk with Henry.  He headed for the tin.  It was

a bit of a nuisance Crumpet getting in the way and hissing at him, what a cheek, he only

wanted his dinner.  He knew Crumpet’s food was too high up for him to reach and

anything on the floor was Wilfrid’s by right.  He avoided the batting claws and settled

down with the tin between his paws.  He sniffed – good it was fish for a change, how

kind of Madge to vary his diet!

Before he could start, he became aware of an itchy feeling around his rump.  Oh no, not

fleas again, they were such a nuisance.  He turned round to scratch.  When he turned back

the tin had inexplicably rolled away and he had to get up and reposition himself.  Blow,

there was that itch again; he’d have to scratch until the flea gave up.  Well a flea might

have given up but Fillip didn’t!  As he tickled Wilf’s rump so Butts and Claude and a

couple of spoons kicked their feeble little kicks at the tin of cat food.  Once again Wilfrid

was nonplussed to find the tin had moved away.  It took several manoeuvres before the

truth dawned on him!  His friends were trying to starve him to death!  He whirled round

just in time to see Fillip position his prongs for another tickle, “Fiwip, what you doing to

Wilfie’s wump?  You’re stopping me eating my dinner!”

“It’s not YOUR dinner Wilf – its Crumpets we’re trying to stop you taking it.”

“Why?” Wilf was bewildered “It’s on the floor, so it’s mine and I’ll starve if I don’t eat

it!”

“No you won’t Wilf, it’s not meant for you, Madge will be in soon to feed you.

“Wilfy can’t wait that long, Wilfy dying of hunger!”

Crumpet had watched the attempts of the kitchen Crew to help him with some amazement mixed with suspicion.

“Why do you APPEAR to be helping me?” he asked “What are you really up to  – and

whatever it is stop it now!”

“Don’t be daft Crumpet!” said Fillip wearily.  “We’re just trying to help, whether you

believe it or not!  Honestly we’re not up to anything – don’t you want us to save your

dinner?”

Crumpet was not convinced, for one thing if he were to believe Fillip he would have to be

grateful, maybe even do something in return and that would not only be humiliating but

could be inconvenient too!  Testing the ground Crumpet batted Butts under the cooker.

“I bet you don’t want to help me now!” he purred.

Fillip gritted his teeth as he helped Butts out, “Yes we do Crumpet!  Look, we all live

together in this kitchen, why can’t you co-operate with us, its much more fun than being

enemies!”

All the while this conversation had been going on Wilf was trying desperately to get hold

of the tin of food and demolish it before Madge came back and robbed him of it.  He

didn’t like the way things were going at all.  He loved Crumpet, but, food was food and it

was every dog for himself.

“All right,” said Crumpet, “just this once, if I roll the tin under the cooker, far enough to

be out of Wilfrid’s reach, will you promise to roll it out again when Madge comes back?”

“That’s a good idea, yes we’ll do that for you Crumpet – old friend!”

With another disbelieving look Crumpet turned his attention to his tin, which a couple of

spoons were kicking towards the cooker.  Crumpet took over and finished the job,

watching in trepidation as his dinner rolled out of sight and out of reach.

Eventually Madge came back into the kitchen.  She opened a tin of meat for Wilfrid (Just

in time to stop him dropping down dead from starvation he thought!)  As she went into

the larder for ingredients for Henry’s dinner the Kitchen Crew, those who were not

exhausted from previous efforts, lay down and with their little feet kicked Crumpet’s tin

out from under the cooker.  Madge turned round just in time to see Crumpet repossess it

and look up at her with an injured expression and a plaintive eye.  Madge was so

apologetic when she had set him up with his meal properly in his dish she gave him more

milk than usual as compensation.

“Thanks guys!” said Crumpet to the Kitchen Crew.  They were delighted.

title: A Shocking Time!
body:
Having Wilfrid around was not all sweetness and light. He had a lovely nature, gentle and

sweet tempered, he never reacted to Crumpet’s claws with anything but playfulness – he

thought everyone was like him and that Crumpet was just playing rough – he, Wilfrid

could take it!  But he did have one or two bad habits.  One – he was usually hungry and

would grab anything edible that came within his reach and Two – he often got bored.

Not having much of a brain to think about things he would look around for something to

do.  Sometimes watching the Kitchen Crew would occupy him but usually he wanted to

do something with his mouth.  The next best thing to eating with it was chewing. When

he was really young he had chewed rubbish bags to pieces, he had also ruined Henry’s

slippers and Madge’s sandals.  As they learned just how much of a handful Wilfrid was

they also learned not to leave anything chewable around.  Even Madge, forgetful as she

was now took a quick look around the kitchen before leaving Wilfrid there. He had plenty

of things he was allowed to chew like bits of special hide, rubber rings and a fake bone –

even a real bone sometimes.  But stolen sweets are tastier for dogs anyway and he was

always on the lookout for something new.

Once he had caught a glimpse of things in the garden through Crumpets ‘s cat flap that

would be ideal but try as he might he was too big and beefy to get through the cat flap

and had twice got stuck.  Crumpet was outraged to have his personal door invaded by the

great galumph and had given Wilf a real battering with claws out.  Even Wilf thought this

playfulness was going too far and when he had been rescued by Henry decided not to try

again, for a while.

And then the unthinkable happened. Henry left his laptop computer on the table where he

had been working in Madge’s company, which he enjoyed.  It was out of Wilfrid’s reach

so he hadn’t thought twice about leaving it there and he plugged it in to charge the battery

while he and Madge were out. He would finish his work when he got back.  The problem

arose because the socket into which the cable was plugged was not far off the ground and

the cable itself coiled its way across the floor to it.

Wilfrid was bored as usual when Madge was not making a fuss of him, Crumpet had

deposited his weekly shrew on the patio and been rewarded with extra rations which he

was sleeping off in the greenhouse.  So there was only the Kitchen Crew to keep Wilf

occupied.  They were all rather tired and sleepy.  It was Saturday morning and they

always felt relaxed and dozy after Henry had been through thoroughly washing them and

putting them away.  In response to Wilf’s pleas to come down and play with him they had

turned deaf ears.  So Wilfrid had to amuse himself, which was when he spotted the soft

juicy cable lying appetisingly on the floor.  He settled down for a good chew.

Why was it that the laws of physics did not apply to Wilfrid we will never know but he

chewed through the soft rubber to the not so chewy metal in the middle and walked away

without realising he should have gone up in smoke!  So, there in the kitchen, waiting for

Madge was a potential death trap.  If she came in barefoot as she sometimes did and did

not notice the exposed wires of the cable she could easily step on it. At best she would

get a bad burn at worst she could get a shock which would blow her across the room and

possibly kill her.

It was half an hour later when Butts climbed out of his drawer to stretch his weedy little

legs.  He yawned his way over to Wilfrid who was starring into space thinking of nothing

in particular, except for the occasional thought of where and when his next meal would

appear.

“Watcha doin’ Wilf?” said Butts in his best Wilma accent.

“Nufin” said Wilf, “There’s nothing to chew ‘cept that black thing and that tasted funny!”

Butts looked at where Wilf pointed with his nose and he went cold with horror!

“Fillip, Fillip, quick, come and look at this,” he called.  “Wilf’s really done it this time!”

Fillip was duly horrified too and they spent a few minutes thinking of the awful things

which could happen.  Butts was running round in circles wringing his hands.

“We know we can’t put the switch off,” he said “And I don’t think Ted can help this time

either.  How can we warn Madge and Henry?”

“We could point to it, like we did to the unexploded rocket,” said Peter Pallet.

“We could cover it up like we did the chip pan!” said Wilma.

“Wilfrid or Crumpet could help!” said Mable Maple the smallest wooden spoon.

“No, no!” said Fillip “there’s something different about this one.  The other dangers we

dealt with we were able to delay.  I think the only thing we can do with this is to let it

happen but before Madge or Henry get hurt.

“How can we do that?” asked Butts, “I don’t know much about electricity except it is

dangerous!  How Wilf survived I can’t imagine!

“Either he has a guardian angel – or it’s the luck of the Irish or something!  Are you Irish

Wilf?”

I don’t fink so, Filwip, my family was German – is there such a thing as the luck of the

Germans?”

“Let’s get on!” said Fillip, “Madge and Henry could be back at any time.  Now what we

want is something metal to fall on the exposed wires, preferably just as Madge and Henry

come in so they see what’s happened.”

“Well most of us have metal about us,” said Butts and we can’t be killed like humans, but

we might be damaged!  We have to have a volunteer!”

There was silence around the kitchen.  No one wanted to be first to volunteer. Their

sacrifice would probably be accepted by the others with relief.   No one knew what

damage there might be and no one wanted to risk it!

At that moment Crumpet arrived via his cat flap and looked around at the glum faces of

the Kitchen Crew.  He had been much more – not friendly exactly – perhaps conciliatory

towards the Kitchen Crew since they had saved his dinner from Wilf.

“What’s up?” he asked.  Fillip explained.  Crumpet looked thoughtful.  “Electricity is

dangerous,” he agreed “I once saw Henry cut through a ught of, and what he said was not

only brilliant but self sacrificing too!

Crumpet coughed diffidently, the kitchen Crew looked up at him.

“I would like to make a suggestion,” he began pompously.  “I understand why you

utensils though made of metal don’t want to risk damage. So why not use something

metal, not enlivened and damaged already?”  They waited expectantly.  Fillip frowned,

had Crumpet taken on his old spiteful ways?  Was he teasing them?   Then Crumpet, with

a swift bat of his paw knocked his dish to the floor!  Yes it was metal, not enlivened and

damaged – dented when the Kitchen Crew had alerted Madge to a thief with it.  There

was a universal gasp of admiration, Crumpet glowed!  They gathered round him as he

jumped down and started batting his dish towards the damaged cable.  An inch from it he

stopped.  They waited until the last minute, then as Madge and Henry entered the kitchen,

Crumpet batted his dish onto the exposed wires.

There was a bang and a flash!  Madge screamed.  Henry shouted, Crumpet fled to a dark

corner and Wilf trembled – “It wasn’t me!” he assured Madge.  Madge and Henry didn’t

know who to be cross with first but settled for cuddling Crumpet and Wilf instead, they

were so glad they hadn’t been hurt, the cat food dish was a right off.

“At least,” observed Crumpet smugly to himself, “I’ll get a new dinner dish, I didn’t like

that dented one anyway!   Being friends with the kitchen Crew has its compensations –

maybe I’ll go along with them in future. Things have certainly livened up since they

became animated, who knows what they’ll get up to next?”
